While hyperoxia and ICP effects of PEEP are valid concerns, the immediate danger here is the degree of hypocapnia and its ischaemic consequences.

text: Q120 Domain: 1.1 | Subdomain: Patient Examination, Investigation & Diagnosis | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium You are receiving a referral call about a 34-year-old woman, 32 weeks pregnant, admitted to a district general hospital following a witnessed tonic-clonic seizure. BP is 172/110 mmHg. She has no prior history of epilepsy. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management at the referring centre prior to your arrival?

options.A: Loading dose of phenytoin 15–20 mg/kg IV
options.B: Diazepam 10 mg IV as a one-off dose
options.C: Magnesium sulphate 4g IV loading dose over 5–10 minutes
options.D: Labetalol 200mg orally
options.E: Urgent CT head prior to any treatment

explanation: This clinical picture is consistent with eclampsia — new-onset seizure(s) in association with pre-eclampsia (BP ≥140/90 mmHg + proteinuria, or severe features). NICE NG133 (Hypertension in Pregnancy, 2019) and RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 10A (Management of Severe Pre-eclampsia/Eclampsia, updated 2022) both recommend magnesium sulphate as first-line treatment for eclampsia — 4g IV loading dose over 5 minutes, followed by infusion of 1g/hour for 24 hours. It is superior to diazepam and phenytoin for seizure recurrence prevention in eclampsia and has a better fetal/neonatal safety profile. Phenytoin is not recommended for eclampsia in UK/Irish practice. CT head may be required to exclude intracranial pathology but should not delay treatment in the context of clear eclamptic presentation. Antihypertensive treatment is also needed but labetalol would typically be given IV for severe hypertension in this context.

text: Q121 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium You are planning the retrieval of a 67-year-old man with a STEMI-equivalent occlusion on ECG at a hospital 90 minutes by road from the nearest primary PCI centre. He presented 45 minutes ago with ongoing chest pain. The referring hospital has thrombolysis available but no catheter laboratory. What is the most appropriate revascularisation strategy?

options.A: Transfer immediately for primary PCI, accepting the anticipated door-to-balloon time
options.B: Administer thrombolysis at the referring hospital, then transfer for angiography regardless of response
options.C: Administer thrombolysis, reassess at 90 minutes; transfer for rescue PCI if unsuccessful
options.D: Administer thrombolysis only if transfer time exceeds 120 minutes from first medical contact
options.E: Transfer for primary PCI only if symptom onset is within 3 hours

explanation: ESC guidelines on STEMI management (2023) recommend that primary PCI is the preferred reperfusion strategy when it can be performed within 120 minutes of first medical contact (FMC). If primary PCI cannot be achieved within this window, pharmacological reperfusion with thrombolysis should be administered, provided there are no contraindications — as is the case here where the patient presented 45 minutes ago and transfer time is 90 minutes, making FMC-to-balloon time likely to exceed 120 minutes. BCIS/NICOR and the British Cardiovascular Society endorse this pathway. If thrombolysis is given, the patient should be transferred to a PCI-capable centre. If thrombolysis is unsuccessful (failure to achieve >50% ST resolution at 60–90 minutes), rescue PCI should be performed as soon as possible. Option B describes routine pharmaco-invasive strategy which differs from rescue PCI and is also acceptable but is a separate planned approach.

text: Q122 Domain: 1.4 | Subdomain: Preparation for Retrieval | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium Which of the following is the most important physiological target to achieve before commencing road retrieval of a patient with severe traumatic brain injury (GCS 7, bilateral fixed dilated pupils following head CT)?

options.A: SpO₂ ≥94%, MAP ≥65 mmHg, PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa
options.B: SpO₂ ≥98%, MAP ≥80 mmHg, PaCO₂ 3.5–4.0 kPa
options.C: SpO₂ ≥94%, CPP ≥50 mmHg, PaCO₂ 3.5–4.0 kPa
options.D: SpO₂ ≥94%, MAP ≥80 mmHg, PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa
options.E: SpO₂ ≥98%, MAP ≥70 mmHg, PaCO₂ 4.0–4.5 kPa

explanation:
The Brain Trauma Foundation (4th edition) and NICE CG176 define the key physiological targets for severe TBI:
•	SpO₂ should be maintained ≥94% — hypoxia (SpO₂ <90% or PaO₂ <8 kPa) is an independent predictor of poor outcome
•	MAP ≥80 mmHg is recommended in severe TBI (higher than standard sepsis targets) to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP − ICP); a MAP of ≥65 is insufficient in the presence of raised ICP
•	PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa prevents harmful cerebral vasoconstriction while avoiding hypercapnia-induced vasodilation and ICP rises The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (BMJ Publishing) and Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine both emphasise the concept of "normalising physiology before departure" — particularly targeting eucarbia, euoxia, and adequate MAP — as the most modifiable factors to reduce secondary brain injury during transport.

text: Q123 Domain: 1.1 | Subdomain: Patient Examination, Investigation & Diagnosis | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Easy You receive a referral for a 22-year-old male with no past medical history who was found unresponsive at home. On examination at the referring hospital: GCS 7 (E2V2M3), pinpoint pupils, RR 6, SpO₂ 84% on air. Which diagnosis best accounts for this clinical picture and what is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.A: Benzodiazepine overdose; flumazenil 200 mcg IV
options.B: Opioid toxidrome; naloxone 400 mcg IV/IM titrated to response
options.C: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose; sodium bicarbonate 1–2 mmol/kg IV
options.D: Hypoglycaemia; 50 mL 50% glucose IV
options.E: Carbon monoxide poisoning; high-flow oxygen via non-rebreathe mask

explanation: The triad of reduced conscious level, pinpoint (miotic) pupils, and respiratory depression is the classic opioid toxidrome. In the UK/Ireland, opioid toxicity is increasingly encountered in the retrieval setting — both prescription opioids and illicit substances (including fentanyl analogues). JRCALC guidelines (2022) and TOXBASE (National Poisons Information Service) recommend naloxone as the specific antidote — initial dose of 400 mcg IV/IM, titrated to restore adequate respiration. Care should be taken to avoid precipitating acute withdrawal in opioid-dependent individuals; smaller titrated doses (100–200 mcg) may be appropriate. Given the severity of respiratory depression here (RR 6, SpO₂ 84%), airway adjuncts and assisted ventilation may be required before or alongside naloxone. Flumazenil is not first-line even in suspected benzodiazepine overdose due to seizure risk and short duration of action.

text: Q124 Domain: 1.2 | Subdomain: Clinical Data Interpretation | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Hard A ventilated patient with ARDS (P/F ratio 120) is being retrieved. Current settings: PC-CMV, FiO₂ 0.8, RR 22, PC above PEEP 14 cmH₂O, PEEP 12 cmH₂O, I:E 1:2. Measured exhaled Vt is 7.8 mL/kg predicted body weight. Plateau pressure is 34 cmH₂O. What is the single most important ventilator adjustment before departure?

options.A: Increase PEEP to 16 cmH₂O
options.B: Reduce FiO₂ to 0.6
options.C: Reduce tidal volume to achieve 4–6 mL/kg PBW
options.D: Change I:E ratio to 1:1 to improve oxygenation
options.E: Increase RR to 28 to compensate for reduced tidal volume

explanation: The ARDSNet protocol (NEJM 2000) and the ICS/FICM ARDS guidelines recommend lung-protective ventilation with tidal volumes of 4–6 mL/kg predicted body weight (PBW) and plateau pressure <30 cmH₂O. This patient has a Vt of 7.8 mL/kg — above the safe threshold — and a plateau pressure of 34 cmH₂O, which exceeds the target. Volutrauma and barotrauma are the primary drivers of ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) in ARDS. Reducing Vt is the priority. PEEP optimisation is also important in ARDS but secondary to Vt reduction. FiO₂ reduction is appropriate when oxygenation allows but should not be the primary intervention here. In the retrieval setting, the Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine specifically highlights that pre-departure optimisation of ventilator settings to lung-protective targets is a patient safety priority, particularly as access to the ventilator during transport is limited.

text: Q125 Domain: 1.4 | Subdomain: Preparation for Retrieval | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A 75-year-old patient with a GCS of 12 and suspected aspiration pneumonia is being prepared for inter-hospital transfer. She has a nasogastric tube in situ for medication administration. She is not intubated. Which of the following most accurately reflects the RCoA/AAGBI guidance regarding the threshold for intubation prior to transfer?

options.A: GCS ≤8 mandates intubation prior to any transfer
options.B: GCS ≤10 mandates intubation prior to any transfer
options.C: GCS alone is insufficient to mandate intubation; clinical trajectory and airway assessment are required
options.D: Intubation should be deferred to the receiving centre to avoid pre-hospital risk
options.E: An NG tube provides adequate airway protection during transfer

explanation: While GCS ≤8 is often quoted as a threshold for airway intervention, the RCoA (formerly AAGBI) Guidelines for the Provision of Anaesthetic Services for Transfer Medicine, and the FICM/ICS Guidelines for the Transport of the Critically Ill Adult (3rd edition, 2019) are clear that GCS alone is not a mandate for intubation. Clinical trajectory is critical — a patient with GCS 12 but rapidly deteriorating and with impaired airway reflexes may need intubation, while a patient with a stable GCS of 7 due to postictal state with intact airway reflexes may not. Key considerations include: ability to maintain own airway, presence of intact gag/cough reflex, likelihood of deterioration en route, duration and nature of transfer, and available interventional capability during transport. An NG tube does not protect the airway. The decision to intubate should be made by a senior clinician accounting for all these factors, not a single GCS threshold.

text: Q126 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium You are coordinating the retrieval of a 44-year-old woman with community-acquired septic shock (BP 78/45, HR 128, lactate 5.2 mmol/L, temp 39.8°C) from a district general hospital with a 90-minute transfer time to the regional ICU. She has not yet received antibiotics. In terms of antibiotic timing, which statement most accurately reflects current guidance?

options.A: Antibiotics should be withheld until blood cultures are confirmed positive to enable source identification
options.B: Antibiotics should be given within 1 hour of sepsis recognition regardless of transport planning
options.C: Antibiotics can be delayed until arrival at the receiving ICU if transfer is within 90 minutes
options.D: Antibiotics should be given only after imaging confirms the source of infection
options.E: Two sets of blood cultures must be obtained before any antibiotic administration

explanation: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign (SSC) 2021 guidelines and NICE NG51 (Sepsis: Recognition, Diagnosis and Early Management, updated 2023) both recommend antibiotic administration within 1 hour of recognition of sepsis, and within 3 hours for suspected sepsis. In septic shock — defined as vasopressor-requiring hypotension and lactate >2 mmol/L — the evidence is strongest for early antibiotic administration; each hour of delay is associated with increased mortality. Blood cultures should be obtained before antibiotics if this can be done without delay (<45 minutes), but should not delay antibiotic administration. A 90-minute transfer without antibiotics would be clinically unacceptable in this scenario. The retrieval team and referring clinician should ensure empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics are administered at the referring hospital, ideally before or immediately at the time of the retrieval team's arrival. HSCP guidance in Ireland (HSE Sepsis Programme) mirrors SSC guidance in this regard.

text: Q127 Domain: 1.4 | Subdomain: Preparation for Retrieval | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium When calculating oxygen requirements for a road retrieval expected to take 90 minutes, which formula correctly estimates the minimum oxygen volume required?

options.A: Minute volume × FiO₂ × transfer time only
options.B: (Minute volume × FiO₂ × transfer time) + 30–50% safety reserve
options.C: (Minute volume × FiO₂ × transfer time) × 2
options.D: Total oxygen flow rate in L/min × transfer time in minutes, without safety reserve
options.E: Cylinder size divided by estimated flow rate, with no additional calculation required

explanation: The FICM/ICS Guidelines for the Transport of the Critically Ill Adult (3rd edition, 2019) and the ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (BMJ, 2015) both specify that oxygen requirements must be calculated to include the estimated consumption during transfer plus a safety reserve of at least 30–50% to account for delays, unexpected clinical events, and equipment issues. For a ventilated patient the calculation is: MV (L/min) × FiO₂ × transfer time (min). For a patient with MV of 8 L/min, FiO₂ 0.5, 90-minute transfer: 8 × 0.5 × 90 = 360 L minimum + 30–50% reserve = 468–540 L. A size D cylinder holds approximately 340 L, a size E approximately 680 L. Underestimating oxygen requirements is a well-documented cause of critical incidents during retrieval and is specifically addressed in the FICM/ICS guidelines. The safety reserve is essential and non-negotiable.

text: Q128 Domain: 1.1 | Subdomain: Patient Examination, Investigation & Diagnosis | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A 50-year-old male is referred with sudden-onset severe headache described as "the worst of my life," onset at rest, 8/10 severity, with associated neck stiffness. CT head performed 3 hours after onset is reported as normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.A: MRI brain with gadolinium
options.B: CT cerebral angiography immediately
options.C: Lumbar puncture, to be performed at least 12 hours after headache onset
options.D: Repeat CT head in 6 hours
options.E: Discharge with safety-netting as CT is negative

explanation: The clinical presentation is consistent with subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) until proven otherwise. While CT head is highly sensitive for SAH within 6 hours of onset (sensitivity approximately 98% within 6 hours — Perry et al., JAMA 2011), sensitivity drops significantly after this window. NICE CG176 and the RCEM Best Practice Guideline on SAH recommend lumbar puncture (LP) if CT is negative and the headache onset was more than 6 hours ago, to look for xanthochromia — the gold-standard test. LP should ideally be performed at least 12 hours after headache onset to allow adequate time for xanthochromia to develop (bilirubin from haemolysis of red cells). In the retrieval context, a patient with suspected SAH and a negative CT should not be transferred before LP has been performed and reviewed unless there is another compelling clinical reason. CT angiography is appropriate after confirming SAH but not before LP in this scenario.

text: Q129 Domain: 1.2 | Subdomain: Clinical Data Interpretation | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A ventilated patient has the following chest X-ray findings on pre-retrieval review: endotracheal tube tip at 3 cm above the carina, right-sided opacification with air bronchograms, tracheal deviation to the right, and a right-sided pleural effusion. The patient's SpO₂ is 91% on FiO₂ 0.6. What is the most likely diagnosis and immediate management priority?

options.A: Left-sided tension pneumothorax; immediate needle decompression on the left
options.B: Right-sided consolidation with pleural effusion; increase FiO₂ and continue transfer
options.C: Right main bronchus intubation with left lung atelectasis; withdraw ETT 2–3 cm
options.D: Left lower lobe collapse; physiotherapy and transfer
options.E: Right-sided haemothorax; insert chest drain before transfer

explanation: Right main bronchus (RMB) intubation is the most common intubation complication due to the more acute angle of the right main bronchus relative to the trachea. It produces left lung atelectasis with the following features: tracheal deviation toward the left (not the right), and ipsilateral right-sided hyperinflation. However, the ETT tip position of 3 cm above the carina combined with right-sided opacification raises an important alternative — if the tracheal deviation is to the right (away from the opacification), this suggests volume loss on the right, which could indicate left RMB intubation. The pre-retrieval CXR review is a core safety check; the FICM/ICS guidance and the Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine both identify ETT position verification as mandatory prior to transfer. In the context of right opacification with rightward tracheal deviation, RMB intubation of the right bronchus causing left lung atelectasis with compensatory rightward shift is more anatomically consistent; withdrawing the ETT by 2–3 cm and reassessing is the safest immediate step.

text: Q130 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Hard A 38-year-old woman with known Type 1 diabetes is retrieved in diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). ABG: pH 7.09, bicarbonate 8 mmol/L, glucose 34 mmol/L, ketones 4.8 mmol/L, potassium 3.1 mmol/L. She is drowsy but maintaining her airway. According to JBDS-IP guidelines, what is the most critical electrolyte management issue during her transfer?

options.A: Administer sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidosis
options.B: Ensure potassium replacement is ongoing before and during insulin infusion
options.C: Administer hypertonic saline for the hyponatraemia
options.D: Withhold insulin until potassium is above 5.0 mmol/L
options.E: Administer dextrose immediately to prevent hypoglycaemia during insulin infusion

explanation: The Joint British Diabetes Societies (JBDS) Inpatient Care Group guideline on DKA management (2021 update) is explicit: insulin infusion drives potassium into cells, worsening hypokalaemia, which can cause fatal arrhythmias. Potassium replacement must be initiated before or concurrent with insulin if serum K⁺ <3.5 mmol/L — in this case K⁺ is 3.1 mmol/L. The JBDS protocol states: do not start fixed rate insulin infusion (FRIII) if K⁺ <3.5 mmol/L; replace potassium first and recheck. Sodium bicarbonate is not recommended in DKA unless pH <6.9 and is specifically advised against in standard DKA management due to risks of paradoxical CSF acidosis, hypokalaemia, and delayed recovery. Dextrose-containing fluids are introduced later when glucose <14 mmol/L to allow continued insulin administration. In the retrieval context, ensuring ongoing potassium replacement and frequent monitoring during transfer is critical.

text: Q131 Domain: 1.4 | Subdomain: Preparation for Retrieval | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Easy Which of the following statements most accurately describes appropriate vascular access requirements prior to inter-hospital retrieval of a critically ill adult patient?

options.A: A single peripheral IV cannula is adequate for most retrievals if patent and large-bore
options.B: At least two large-bore peripheral IVs or one central venous catheter should be secured and confirmed patent before departure
options.C: An arterial line is mandatory for all retrievals
options.D: Intraosseous access is an acceptable primary route for all medications during long-distance transfers
options.E: Central venous access should always be established at the referring hospital before transfer

explanation: The FICM/ICS Guidelines for the Transport of the Critically Ill Adult (3rd edition, 2019) stipulate that adequate, reliable vascular access must be established and confirmed patent prior to departure. The minimum standard for a critically ill patient is at least two functioning large-bore peripheral IV cannulae or one functioning CVC. Access that is adequate at the referring hospital may become unreliable during transfer due to patient movement, patient position, and tube/line tension in confined spaces. Establishing additional access in a moving vehicle or aircraft is technically difficult and potentially dangerous. An arterial line is strongly recommended for haemodynamically unstable patients and those requiring vasoactive infusions, but is not categorically mandatory for all retrievals. Intraosseous access is appropriate for emergency use but is not recommended as the primary vascular access route for extended transfers due to flow rate limitations and risk of dislodgement.

text: Q132 Domain: 1.1 | Subdomain: Patient Examination, Investigation & Diagnosis | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A 29-year-old male is referred with a 3-day history of fever, productive cough, and pleuritic chest pain. He is hypoxic (SpO₂ 88% on 15L O₂), tachycardic (HR 124), hypotensive (BP 92/60), confused (GCS 13), and has a urea of 14 mmol/L. Using the CURB-65 score, what is his score and what does this indicate regarding immediate management?

options.A: CURB-65 score 3; suggests moderate risk, outpatient or short-stay treatment appropriate
options.B: CURB-65 score 4; indicates severe CAP, requires hospital admission and consideration of ICU
options.C: CURB-65 score 5; indicates severe CAP with high mortality risk, ICU admission required
options.D: CURB-65 score 2; indicates low risk, oral antibiotics sufficient
options.E: CURB-65 score 4; IV antibiotics recommended but transfer not required

explanation:
CURB-65 (NICE CG191, BTS CAP Guideline 2009, updated 2023 via BTS):
•	Confusion (GCS <15): 1 point ✓ (GCS 13)
•	Urea >7 mmol/L: 1 point ✓ (urea 14)
•	Respiratory rate ≥30/min: requires checking — assume 1 point in context of SpO₂ 88% on 15L O₂
•	Blood pressure <90 systolic or ≤60 diastolic: 1 point ✓ (BP 92/60 — diastolic ≤60)
•	65 years or older: 0 points ✗
Score = 4–5. A score of ≥3 indicates severe CAP with 30-day mortality of 17–57%. NICE CG191 recommends hospital admission and assessment for intensive care for scores ≥3. This patient also meets criteria for sepsis and likely septic shock, and should be considered for ICU-level care and retrieval to a centre with this capability. The BTS guideline recommends prompt IV antibiotics (dual therapy with beta-lactam + macrolide) and consideration of respiratory support.

text: Q133 Domain: 1.2 | Subdomain: Clinical Data Interpretation | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium During pre-retrieval assessment, you review the echocardiogram of a patient being transferred for cardiogenic shock following anterior STEMI. It shows an ejection fraction of 20%, moderate mitral regurgitation, and a dilated, hypokinetic left ventricle. The patient is currently on noradrenaline 0.3 mcg/kg/min and has a MAP of 62 mmHg. Which vasopressor/inotrope combination is most appropriate for this patient's haemodynamic profile during transfer?

options.A: Increase noradrenaline to 0.5 mcg/kg/min
options.B: Add vasopressin 0.04 units/min
options.C: Add dobutamine 5–10 mcg/kg/min to the existing noradrenaline
options.D: Switch noradrenaline to adrenaline (epinephrine) infusion
options.E: Add metaraminol boluses for blood pressure support

explanation: Cardiogenic shock with a severely reduced ejection fraction (EF 20%) requires a combination approach: vasopressor support to maintain perfusion pressure AND inotropic support to improve cardiac output. The ESC 2021 guidelines on acute heart failure, and the EuroShock registry data support noradrenaline as first-line vasopressor (preferred over dopamine — SOAP-II trial). However, in cardiogenic shock with low EF and evidence of pump failure, adding dobutamine (beta-1 agonist, positive inotrope) at 5–10 mcg/kg/min is appropriate to augment cardiac output. This combination — noradrenaline + dobutamine — is the most evidence-based approach in cardiogenic shock. Pure vasopressors alone (increasing noradrenaline, adding vasopressin) will raise afterload without improving contractility, potentially worsening output. Adrenaline has inotropic and vasopressor properties but increases lactate and heart rate, with less favourable evidence in cardiogenic shock.

text: Q134 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A 72-year-old patient with acute-on-chronic liver failure is referred for retrieval. He is encephalopathic (Grade III), coagulopathic (INR 3.2), and thrombocytopaenic (platelets 48 × 10⁹/L). A peripheral IV has been attempted four times unsuccessfully. You are considering central venous access. Which site is most appropriate and what precaution is most important?

options.A: Subclavian vein; most familiar route, coagulopathy is not a contraindication
options.B: Femoral vein; preferred in coagulopathic patients as it is compressible if bleeding occurs
options.C: Internal jugular vein with ultrasound guidance; preferred in coagulopathy
options.D: Any site is acceptable providing INR is corrected to <1.5 with FFP first
options.E: Intraosseous access is preferable to CVC in all coagulopathic patients

explanation: In coagulopathic patients (INR >2, platelets <50 × 10⁹/L), the femoral vein is the preferred site for central venous access because it is the most compressible site if haemorrhage occurs post-procedure. The subclavian vein carries the highest risk of uncontrollable haemorrhage (non-compressible) and pneumothorax in coagulopathic patients and should be avoided. The internal jugular vein with ultrasound guidance is the preferred non-femoral site in most patients, but the femoral site is favoured when coagulopathy is severe. The RCoA/AAGBI guidance and NICE CG174 (Central Venous Access) do not mandate INR correction before CVC insertion in all cases — the decision depends on the urgency and severity of coagulopathy. Blanket INR correction with FFP is not supported by NICE CG174 and may be inappropriate in ACLF where FFP may worsen portal hypertension. IO access is appropriate for emergency resuscitation but not for infusions of hepatotoxic or vesicant drugs used in ICU care.

text: Q135 Domain: 1.4 | Subdomain: Preparation for Retrieval | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A patient is being retrieved by road with ongoing noradrenaline and propofol infusions. Which of the following is the most important consideration regarding infusion pumps for this transfer?

options.A: All syringe drivers should be switched off during loading and restarted once in transit
options.B: Battery life should be confirmed and syringe volume assessed to last the full transfer plus safety reserve; simultaneous pump changeovers should be avoided for vasopressors
options.C: Only mains-powered pumps should be used during transfers
options.D: Infusions should be consolidated to a single pump to simplify management
options.E: Vasopressor infusions can be paused for up to 10 minutes during loading without clinical consequence

explanation: The FICM/ICS Transport Guidelines (3rd edition, 2019) address medication infusion safety specifically. Key principles include: (1) confirm battery life of all syringe drivers before departure — sufficient for transfer duration plus reserve; (2) ensure adequate syringe/bag volume for the full journey; (3) never simultaneously change vasopressor syringe drivers — stagger changes to prevent haemodynamic instability from vasopressor interruption; (4) vasopressor infusions must not be paused during loading or transit — even brief interruptions can cause precipitous haemodynamic collapse. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine highlights vasopressor-related critical incidents as a significant source of in-transit morbidity. Consolidating infusions to a single pump in a critically ill patient is hazardous and risks drug interactions and flow-rate errors. Mains power is not always available; battery-powered operation is the norm in transport.

text: Q136 Domain: 1.1 | Subdomain: Patient Examination, Investigation & Diagnosis | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Easy When taking a retrieval referral call, which structured communication tool is most widely recommended for the clinical handover between referring and retrieval team?

options.A: ATMIST
options.B: SBAR
options.C: MIST
options.D: ISBAR
options.E: RSVP

explanation: ISBAR (Identity, Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) is the recommended structured communication tool in Irish and UK healthcare for clinical handovers, endorsed by the HSE (Ireland), NHS England, and the Royal College of Physicians of Ireland (RCPI). It is specifically recommended in the HSE National Clinical Programme for Deteriorating Patients. In the retrieval context, ISBAR allows the receiving retrieval clinician to rapidly obtain the patient's identity, clinical situation, background history, current assessment, and the specific recommendation being made (i.e., what the caller needs from the retrieval service). SBAR is the original UK/international tool from which ISBAR evolved; the Irish version adds the "Identity" component for patient safety. ATMIST is used for trauma handover (Age, Time, Mechanism, Injuries, Signs, Treatment). MIST is a simplified prehospital handover format. The Faculty of Prehospital Care and MICAS/NAS CCRS use ISBAR as the standard retrieval referral format.

text: Q137 Domain: 1.4 | Subdomain: Preparation for Retrieval | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A patient with severe ARDS (P/F ratio 90) is being retrieved by road. The retrieval team's transport ventilator is limited to a maximum PEEP of 15 cmH₂O and FiO₂ of 1.0. The patient is currently on the ICU ventilator with PEEP 18 cmH₂O, FiO₂ 0.95, prone positioning, and inhaled nitric oxide. Which statement best reflects the decision-making process before this transfer?

options.A: The transfer should proceed immediately as clinical need outweighs technical limitations
options.B: The retrieval team should switch to the transport ventilator at the bedside 30 minutes before departure to assess stability
options.C: The transport ventilator's limitations must be assessed against the patient's clinical needs; specialist ECMO retrieval or a more capable ventilator should be considered if settings cannot be matched
options.D: Prone positioning should be continued during road transport to maintain oxygenation
options.E: Inhaled nitric oxide can be safely discontinued 1 hour before transfer without adverse effect

explanation: The Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine and the FICM/ICS Transport Guidelines both emphasise that the capabilities of the retrieval platform — including ventilator specifications — must be matched to patient clinical needs before committing to transfer. A patient who requires PEEP >15 cmH₂O, high FiO₂, and adjunct therapies (iNO, prone positioning) represents extreme retrieval complexity. If the transport ventilator cannot replicate ICU settings, the patient may deteriorate catastrophically en route. Options include: (1) source a transport ventilator capable of higher PEEP/FiO₂; (2) consider ECMO retrieval (ECMO instituted at referring hospital before transfer); (3) perform a trial on the transport ventilator with acceptable stability before departure. Prone positioning in a road vehicle is not feasible for standard retrievals and carries extreme logistical risk. Inhaled nitric oxide has a rebound pulmonary hypertension effect on abrupt discontinuation and cannot be safely stopped without weaning.

text: Q138 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A 56-year-old male with a known history of haematological malignancy (neutrophil count 0.2 × 10⁹/L) presents with rigors, fever (39.6°C), and hypotension (BP 86/50). What is the most critical time-sensitive intervention?

options.A: Urgent CT to identify source of infection before antibiotics
options.B: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics including antipseudomonal cover within 1 hour of presentation
options.C: G-CSF administration to boost neutrophil count before antibiotics
options.D: Antifungal therapy as first-line given likely fungal aetiology in neutropenia
options.E: Fluid resuscitation only until neutrophil count is known

explanation: Febrile neutropenia (FN) in a patient with absolute neutrophil count <0.5 × 10⁹/L is a medical emergency. NICE CG151 (Neutropenic Sepsis, 2012, updated via evidence review 2022), the European Society for Medical Oncology (ESMO), and the Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA) all mandate empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition. The NICE guideline specifically recommends an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem in high-risk patients) as first-line. Delaying for CT or microbiological results is not acceptable. Antifungals (e.g., micafungin or liposomal amphotericin B) are added empirically in patients with FN who do not respond to 4–7 days of antibiotics, or in high-risk patients per local protocol. In the retrieval context, if antibiotics have not been given at the referring hospital, they must be given before or at the time of departure.

text: Q139 Domain: 1.2 | Subdomain: Clinical Data Interpretation | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium You review a 12-lead ECG of a 48-year-old female referred with central chest pain and dyspnoea. The ECG shows: sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3 pattern, right bundle branch block, and T-wave inversion in V1–V4. What is the most likely diagnosis and most time-critical intervention?

options.A: Anterior STEMI; primary PCI within 90 minutes
options.B: Massive pulmonary embolism; systemic thrombolysis if haemodynamically compromised
options.C: Right ventricular infarction; IV fluids and avoid nitrates
options.D: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; beta-blockade
options.E: Pericarditis; NSAIDs and colchicine

explanation: The combination of S1Q3T3, RBBB, and right heart strain pattern (T-wave inversion V1–V4) is the classic ECG presentation of massive or submassive pulmonary embolism. While this ECG pattern is present in only ~20% of PE cases, its presence alongside haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, dyspnoea) is strongly suggestive. ESC 2019 PE guidelines and NICE NG158 (Venous Thromboembolic Diseases) recommend risk stratification using the Pulmonary Embolism Severity Index (PESI), haemodynamic status, and right heart strain markers. For massive PE (haemodynamic compromise — hypotension, shock), systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100mg over 2 hours) is indicated unless there are major contraindications. Surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis are alternatives. In the retrieval context, a patient with massive PE being transferred to a centre with CTPA and echocardiography capability may deteriorate in transit — the retrieval team must have thrombolysis available and a clear threshold for its use.

text: Q140 Domain: 1.1 | Subdomain: Patient Examination, Investigation & Diagnosis | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A 40-year-old male is referred from a peripheral hospital. He has a history of heavy alcohol use. He presents with confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia. Temperature 37.2°C, GCS 12. Blood glucose is 5.4 mmol/L. Which is the most important immediate intervention?

options.A: Intravenous dextrose 5% infusion
options.B: CT head to exclude intracranial haemorrhage before any treatment
options.C: IV thiamine (Pabrinex) 500 mg three times daily for 3 days before any glucose-containing fluids
options.D: Haloperidol for presumed alcohol withdrawal delirium
options.E: Lorazepam for presumed alcohol-related seizure

explanation: The triad of confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia constitutes Wernicke's Encephalopathy (WE) until proven otherwise in a patient with alcohol use disorder. WE is caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency and is a neurological emergency. The Royal College of Physicians (RCP) guideline on Alcohol-related Brain Damage (2014), NICE CG100 (Alcohol-use disorders: physical complications, updated 2017), and the British National Formulary (BNF) all recommend high-dose IV thiamine (Pabrinex — 2 pairs of ampoules three times daily, each pair containing 500 mg thiamine) before any glucose-containing fluids, as administering glucose in a thiamine-deficient patient can precipitate or worsen Wernicke's by consuming residual thiamine stores. CT head may be required to exclude other pathology but should not delay thiamine administration. Wernicke's may progress to Korsakoff's psychosis — an irreversible amnestic syndrome — if untreated. In the retrieval setting, Pabrinex must be available and administered before transfer.

text: Q141 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Hard A 54-year-old man with hypertension is referred with a sudden-onset tearing interscapular pain radiating to the jaw. BP is 195/105 mmHg in the right arm and 150/90 mmHg in the left arm. CXR shows a widened mediastinum. ECG shows normal sinus rhythm with no ischaemic changes. What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.A: Activate primary PCI pathway for presumed posterior STEMI
options.B: Administer systemic thrombolysis for presumed aortic dissection with distal ischaemia
options.C: Control heart rate and blood pressure with IV labetalol; urgent CT aortography; transfer to cardiothoracic surgical centre
options.D: Administer heparin anticoagulation and arrange urgent echocardiography
options.E: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation for presumed neurogenic shock

explanation: This presentation is highly consistent with acute Type A aortic dissection (Stanford): tearing/ripping chest/back pain, hypertension, BP differential between arms (>20 mmHg in >30% of Type A dissections), and widened mediastinum on CXR. ESC 2014 guidelines on Aortic Diseases and the EACTS/ESVS joint guidelines recommend immediate heart rate and blood pressure control to reduce aortic wall stress (target HR <60 bpm, systolic BP 100–120 mmHg using IV labetalol or esmolol), followed by urgent CT aortography for definitive diagnosis. Type A dissection (involving ascending aorta) requires emergency surgical repair — mortality increases ~1–2% per hour without surgery. Thrombolysis and heparin are absolutely contraindicated (risk of catastrophic haemorrhage into the pericardium or along the false lumen). Fluid resuscitation is not indicated unless there is evidence of haemodynamic compromise from tamponade or free rupture. The retrievalist must prioritise transfer to the nearest cardiothoracic surgical centre without delay.

text: Q142 Domain: 1.4 | Subdomain: Preparation for Retrieval | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to the use of nitrous oxide (Entonox) during air retrieval?

options.A: Patient age over 70 years
options.B: Presence of closed air-containing spaces (e.g., pneumothorax, bowel obstruction, middle ear pathology, recent eye surgery)
options.C: Concurrent use of opioid analgesia
options.D: Transfer duration greater than 60 minutes
options.E: Altitude above 3,000 feet

explanation: Nitrous oxide (N₂O, Entonox) diffuses into gas-containing body cavities far more rapidly than nitrogen diffuses out, causing cavity expansion. Boyle's Law states that at altitude, gas volumes expand as atmospheric pressure falls. The combination of these two effects makes Entonox absolutely contraindicated in the presence of: pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum, bowel obstruction or ileus, air embolism, middle ear pathology (including otitis media), recent intraocular surgery or gas tamponade, recent intracranial surgery, and diving-related decompression illness. This is addressed in the Oxford Handbook of Pre-Hospital Care (Greaves/Porter) and the Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine, as well as by the British Oxygen Company/BOC Entonox product guidance. Altitude alone at 3,000 feet does not contraindicate use, though considerations apply at higher cabin altitudes. Duration of use is relevant due to megaloblastic effects with prolonged exposure but does not constitute an absolute contraindication. Co-administration with opioids is common in pain management and is not contraindicated.

text: Q143 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A 31-year-old female, 28 weeks pregnant, is referred following a high-speed road traffic collision. She is haemodynamically unstable (BP 88/54, HR 132). FAST scan shows free fluid in the abdomen. The patient is conscious and has a CTG showing foetal heart decelerations. What is the most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.A: Urgent caesarean section to deliver the foetus and improve maternal haemodynamics
options.B: Immediate maternal haemorrhage control and resuscitation as the priority; foetal wellbeing is dependent on maternal survival
options.C: Transfer the patient immediately to the nearest obstetric unit without intervention
options.D: Administer tocolytics to stop preterm labour
options.E: Commence fresh frozen plasma only, withholding packed red cells until cross-match is available

explanation: In maternal trauma with haemodynamic instability, the guiding principle endorsed by ATLS (10th edition), RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 56 (Maternal Collapse in Pregnancy), and the Irish Institute of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (IIOG) is: resuscitate the mother first. Foetal wellbeing is primarily dependent on maternal haemodynamic stability — a well-perfused mother is the best foetal resuscitator. Management priorities follow standard ABCDE trauma principles, with modifications for pregnancy (left lateral tilt to relieve aortocaval compression, anticipate difficult airway, higher blood volume). Massive haemorrhage protocol should be activated with 1:1:1 ratio of red cells, FFP, and platelets. Perimortem caesarean section (at 4 minutes in cardiac arrest) has specific indications but is not appropriate here while the mother remains conscious and haemodynamic resuscitation is ongoing. Tocolytics are contraindicated in the setting of haemorrhagic shock.

text: Q144 Domain: 1.2 | Subdomain: Clinical Data Interpretation | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A patient with septic shock is on a noradrenaline infusion of 0.4 mcg/kg/min. Despite adequate fluid resuscitation and antibiotics, his MAP remains 58 mmHg and his lactate is 4.6 mmol/L at 6 hours. ScvO₂ is 58%. What does the ScvO₂ indicate and what additional intervention should be considered?

options.A: ScvO₂ 58% indicates adequate oxygen delivery; no additional intervention required
options.B: ScvO₂ 58% indicates excessive sedation; reduce propofol infusion
options.C: ScvO₂ 58% indicates inadequate oxygen delivery relative to demand; consider dobutamine and/or red cell transfusion if Hb <70 g/L
options.D: ScvO₂ 58% is a normal value in septic shock; continue current management
options.E: ScvO₂ 58% indicates venous hypertension; reduce IV fluid rate

explanation: Central venous oxygen saturation (ScvO₂) <70% indicates that tissue oxygen extraction is exceeding delivery — the tissues are extracting more oxygen than normal because supply is inadequate. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign (2021) and Dellinger's original EGDT work (NEJM 2001) target ScvO₂ ≥70% as a marker of adequate oxygen delivery. Persistent ScvO₂ <70% despite adequate MAP and fluid resuscitation suggests either: (a) low cardiac output requiring inotropic support (dobutamine is the agent of choice — positive inotrope without the renal/splanchnic vasoconstriction of dopamine); or (b) inadequate oxygen-carrying capacity (anaemia) — transfusion to Hb ≥70 g/L (or ≥90 g/L in active cardiac ischaemia) is recommended. SSC 2021 guidelines continue to recommend addressing persistent tissue hypoperfusion markers despite initial resuscitation, with dobutamine as the inotrope of choice in septic shock with cardiac dysfunction.

text: Q145 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Hard A patient has been intubated for airway protection following a paracetamol overdose. INR is 6.8, creatinine is 380 µmol/L, bilirubin is 145 µmol/L, pH is 7.26, and lactate is 5.1 mmol/L. Grade III hepatic encephalopathy is documented. What is the most critical intervention and the most important reason to transfer urgently?

options.A: Initiate N-acetylcysteine (NAC) infusion, transfer to liver transplant centre
options.B: Administer activated charcoal; transfer is not urgently required
options.C: Fresh frozen plasma to correct coagulopathy before transfer
options.D: Haemodialysis at the referring hospital for renal failure before transfer
options.E: IV glucose and lactulose; reassess in 24 hours before transfer decision

explanation:
This patient meets King's College Criteria for acute liver failure (ALF) with features strongly indicating poor prognosis without transplantation:
•	pH <7.30 (pH 7.26)
•	INR >6.5 (INR 6.8)
•	Creatinine >300 µmol/L (creatinine 380)
•	Grade III–IV hepatic encephalopathy
NICE CG183 (Liver conditions), the British Society of Gastroenterology (BSG), and the European Association for the Study of the Liver (EASL) Clinical Practice Guidelines on Acute Liver Failure mandate urgent transfer to a specialist liver unit / transplant centre when King's College Criteria are met. N-acetylcysteine should be continued or initiated at any stage of paracetamol-induced ALF (SNAP trial, Lee et al.) and continued during transfer. Correcting the coagulopathy with FFP before transfer is not indicated unless there is active bleeding — the INR is the most sensitive marker of liver synthetic function and correction masks clinical progression. Charcoal is only useful within 1–2 hours of ingestion. Transfer without delay is the priority; specialist units have the ability to list for transplant and provide organ support (MARS, haemodiafiltration).

text: Q146 Domain: 1.4 | Subdomain: Preparation for Retrieval | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Easy Which of the following documentation items is most important to ensure accompanies a critically ill patient during inter-hospital transfer?

options.A: The patient's GP letter from their most recent consultation
options.B: A written retrieval care record documenting current clinical status, medications, interventions, and relevant investigations; consent/next of kin details; and the retrieval team's contact number
options.C: A printed copy of all previous hospital admissions dating back 5 years
options.D: DNAR order only if the patient has capacity to sign
options.E: No documentation is required if verbal handover is given to the receiving team

explanation: The FICM/ICS Transport Guidelines (3rd edition, 2019) and the HSE Patient Safety frameworks specify that every critically ill transfer must be accompanied by contemporaneous written documentation. This should include: patient identity details, current diagnoses, vital signs and clinical status at time of departure, medications being administered, investigations and results performed at the referring centre, relevant past medical history, advance care plans or DNAR orders, and the contact details of the retrieval team and referring clinician. This documentation is a medico-legal requirement and is essential for continuity of care. The retrieval record serves as the medical notes for the transfer period and should be incorporated into the patient's hospital record at the receiving centre. Sub-optimal documentation during transfers has been identified in multiple critical incident reviews, including the NCEPOD report on Emergency Surgery (2011) and the FICM/ICS audit data.

text: Q147 Domain: 1.3 | Subdomain: Treatment Prioritisation & Planning | Type: Underpinning Knowledge | Difficulty: Medium A 66-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 42% predicted) is referred with an acute exacerbation. ABG on 28% Venturi mask: pH 7.29, PaCO₂ 8.9 kPa, PaO₂ 7.8 kPa, HCO₃⁻ 30 mmol/L. He is drowsy (GCS 13), RR 28, SpO₂ 88%. NIV has not yet been attempted. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.A: Increase oxygen to 60% face mask to improve SpO₂
options.B: Intubate immediately given GCS 13 and respiratory failure
options.C: Initiate non-invasive ventilation (NIV) with IPAP 12–16 cmH₂O, EPAP 4–5 cmH₂O, FiO₂ titrated to SpO₂ 88–92%
options.D: Nebulised bronchodilators only; reassess in 30 minutes
options.E: Palliative care referral given severity of underlying disease

explanation: This patient has acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (Type 2) secondary to AECOPD with decompensated respiratory acidosis (pH 7.29, PaCO₂ 8.9). NICE NG115 (COPD, 2019) and the BTS/ICS NIV guideline (2016) recommend NIV as the first-line treatment for hypercapnic respiratory acidosis in AECOPD — superior to invasive ventilation in this population and associated with reduced intubation rates, ICU length of stay, and mortality. Starting IPAP 12–16 and EPAP 4–5 is appropriate initial NIV titration, increasing IPAP gradually as tolerated. Target SpO₂ in COPD is 88–92% — not higher, as excessive oxygen can suppress hypoxic ventilatory drive and worsen hypercapnia. Increasing FiO₂ without respiratory support would worsen CO₂ retention. Immediate intubation is not indicated while NIV is an option and the patient is cooperative. NIV should be initiated at the referring hospital and may be continued during transfer if the retrieval platform has NIV capability.

text: A 34-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation requires inter-hospital retrieval for severe pre-eclampsia with HELLP syndrome. She is conscious and haemodynamically stable. Which patient positioning is essential during road transport?

options.A: Supine with legs elevated 15° to improve venous return
options.B: Left lateral tilt of 15–30° with right side elevated
options.C: Right lateral tilt of 15–30° with left side elevated
options.D: Sitting upright at 45° to reduce aspiration risk
options.E: Supine flat — positioning does not affect haemodynamics after 20 weeks

explanation: From 20 weeks gestation, the gravid uterus exerts significant pressure on the inferior vena cava (IVC) and descending aorta when the patient is supine — a phenomenon known as aortocaval compression. This can reduce venous return by up to 30%, causing supine hypotensive syndrome, which can precipitate maternal and foetal compromise. Left lateral tilt (right side up, right side elevated) displaces the uterus to the left, relieving IVC compression. This is consistently recommended in RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 10A (Management of Severe Pre-eclampsia), NICE NG133 (Hypertension in Pregnancy 2019), and the Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine. Right lateral tilt (option C) would worsen aortocaval compression. Supine positioning (options A and E) are unsafe beyond 20 weeks. Sitting at 45° (option D) may be tolerated but does not consistently relieve aortocaval compression and reduces the ability to monitor and intervene rapidly in an emergency.

text: A 29-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks presents with a concealed antepartum haemorrhage and suspected placental abruption. Her BP is 90/60 mmHg, HR 128 bpm. She is pallid and anxious. Foetal heart rate trace shows late decelerations. The retrieval team is requested for inter-hospital transfer to a consultant-led obstetric unit. What is the most important clinical principle specific to haemorrhage assessment in late pregnancy?

options.A: Blood loss in abruption is always visible and can be accurately estimated from observed bleeding
options.B: A normal maternal BP effectively excludes significant haemorrhage in pregnancy
options.C: Maternal physiological reserve in pregnancy means blood pressure is often maintained until blood loss approaches 1,500–2,000 ml, making tachycardia an early and significant warning sign
options.D: Haemorrhage in pregnancy is best managed with colloid resuscitation to avoid dilutional coagulopathy
options.E: Foetal heart rate is not a reliable indicator of maternal blood loss

explanation: Pregnant women have a physiologically increased circulating blood volume (approximately 40–50% above baseline by term) and significant cardiovascular reserve. As a result, hypotension is a late and ominous sign — it may not manifest until 30–35% of circulating volume has been lost. Tachycardia, therefore, is often the earliest and most sensitive haemodynamic indicator of significant haemorrhage in pregnancy. The Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine explicitly states: "many pregnant women will maintain their BP until blood loss approaches 2,000 ml; the development of tachycardia is a very significant sign." Option A is incorrect — in placental abruption particularly, blood can be entirely concealed between the placenta and uterine wall, and the degree of concealed haemorrhage is frequently underestimated. Option B incorrectly relies on BP as an early indicator. Option D — crystalloid (Hartmann's or normal saline) remains the initial resuscitation fluid of choice alongside blood products; there is no evidence favouring colloid in obstetric haemorrhage. RCOG Guideline No. 52 (Prevention and Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage) and the Confidential Enquiry into Maternal Deaths (MBRRACE-UK) both highlight the dangers of underestimating concealed haemorrhage.

text: A peri-arrest Caesarean section (perimortem Caesarean delivery) is being considered during a retrieval. A 33-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation has suffered a witnessed cardiac arrest due to massive pulmonary embolism. CPR is in progress. Which of the following statements most accurately reflects the evidence and guidelines for perimortem Caesarean delivery?

options.A: Perimortem Caesarean should only be performed in a fully equipped operating theatre
options.B: The procedure should be initiated at 4 minutes of cardiac arrest and delivery completed by 5 minutes ("4-minute rule"), primarily to save the foetus
options.C: The primary aim is maternal resuscitation — delivery relieves aortocaval compression and improves effectiveness of CPR; foetal survival is a secondary benefit
options.D: The procedure is contraindicated before 28 weeks gestation as foetal viability is uncertain
options.E: Perimortem Caesarean should be deferred until ROSC is achieved to minimise surgical risk

explanation: The indication for perimortem Caesarean delivery (PMCD) is cardiac arrest in a pregnant woman at ≥20 weeks gestation. The primary rationale is maternal — at ≥20 weeks, the uterus significantly compresses the IVC regardless of positioning, reducing the effectiveness of CPR by impairing venous return and cardiac output. Delivering the foetus by Caesarean relieves this compression and dramatically improves CPR effectiveness, increasing the chance of maternal ROSC. The European Resuscitation Council (ERC) 2021 guidelines and RCOG guidance both emphasise this: "the procedure is primarily for maternal benefit, with foetal survival as an additional potential benefit." The "4-minute rule" (option B) — which historically suggested delivery within 4 minutes of arrest — remains a useful guide as a target, but current ERC 2021 guidance recommends not delaying PMCD: the decision should be made immediately when cardiac arrest is confirmed in a woman at ≥20 weeks. In the retrieval setting, PMCD may need to be performed at scene if transport would cause unacceptable delay. Option A is incorrect — it can be performed wherever the arrest occurs. Option D is incorrect — ≥20 weeks is the threshold.

text: A 26-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation is being retrieved following a tonic-clonic seizure consistent with eclampsia. She has received magnesium sulphate 4g IV loading dose and is now on a 1g/hour maintenance infusion. During transport, the retrieval nurse notes the patient appears drowsy and her respiratory rate has fallen to 8 breaths/min. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.A: Reduce the magnesium infusion rate to 0.5g/hour and monitor
options.B: Stop the magnesium infusion, administer 10 ml of 10% calcium gluconate IV, and support ventilation
options.C: Administer IV flumazenil as concurrent benzodiazepine toxicity is the likely cause
options.D: Intubate immediately as magnesium toxicity is irreversible
options.E: Increase the IV fluid rate to improve renal magnesium excretion

explanation: This presentation is consistent with magnesium toxicity. Therapeutic magnesium levels for eclampsia are 2–3.5 mmol/L; toxicity begins to manifest at progressively higher levels: loss of patellar reflexes at ~4–5 mmol/L; respiratory depression at ~5–6.5 mmol/L; cardiac arrest at >7.5 mmol/L. The sequence of toxicity signs — loss of deep tendon reflexes → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest — means respiratory rate of 8/min is an urgent emergency. Management: (1) Stop the magnesium infusion immediately; (2) 10 ml of 10% calcium gluconate IV over 10 minutes — calcium is the specific antidote to magnesium toxicity; (3) Support ventilation (BVM or intubation if required). NICE NG133, RCOG GTG No. 10A and the UK Obstetric Surveillance System guidance all mandate monitoring of reflexes (patellar), respiratory rate (>16/min before each dose), and urine output (>25ml/hr) during magnesium therapy. Flumazenil (option C) reverses benzodiazepines, not magnesium. Reducing (not stopping) the infusion (option A) is insufficient for established respiratory depression. Improving renal excretion (option E) is too slow for an acute emergency.

text: A 4-year-old, 16 kg child is referred for retrieval with suspected meningococcal septicaemia. He is tachycardic (HR 168), with prolonged capillary refill time of 4 seconds, cold peripheries, and mottled skin. He is maintaining his airway with a GCS of 13. What is the correct initial resuscitation fluid bolus and route?

options.A: 10 ml/kg 0.9% normal saline over 5 minutes via peripheral IV
options.B: 20 ml/kg 0.9% normal saline as rapidly as possible via peripheral IV or IO
options.C: 10 ml/kg 4.5% human albumin solution over 30 minutes via peripheral IV
options.D: 20 ml/kg Hartmann's solution over 10 minutes via IO access only
options.E: 5 ml/kg 0.9% normal saline, reassess, then 5 ml/kg, repeat to maximum 20 ml/kg

explanation: This child has signs of decompensated septic shock — cold peripheries, prolonged CRT, mottling, and tachycardia are hallmarks of the compensated-to-decompensated shock continuum. Current APLS (Advanced Paediatric Life Support, 7th edition), NICE sepsis guideline (NG51), and UK Sepsis Trust paediatric guidance recommend an initial bolus of 20 ml/kg isotonic crystalloid (0.9% normal saline or Hartmann's solution) as rapidly as possible (over 5–10 minutes), via peripheral IV or IO access. IO access is entirely appropriate if IV cannot be rapidly obtained in a shocked child — the APLS guideline supports immediate IO insertion after 2 failed peripheral attempts within 1–2 minutes in acute life-threatening illness. Importantly, the FEAST trial raised concerns about bolus fluid resuscitation in sub-Saharan African children with severe febrile illness, but its findings are not generalised to European/Irish paediatric practice where the infrastructure, monitoring, and intensive care capability exist to manage fluid overload. The 5 ml/kg incremental approach (option E) lacks evidence and delays resuscitation. Albumin (option C) is not first-line. The key message: give 20 ml/kg rapidly and reassess.

text: When calculating an endotracheal tube size for a 7-year-old child for retrieval, which formula should be applied, and which ETT characteristics should be considered specific to the retrieval environment?

options.A: ETT internal diameter (mm) = Age/4 + 4; use cuffed ETT; inflate cuff to <20 cmH₂O
options.B: ETT internal diameter (mm) = Age/4 + 4; cuffed ETT preferred; inflate cuff to achieve seal and monitor cuff pressure at <25 cmH₂O; account for altitude effects on cuff pressure
options.C: ETT internal diameter (mm) = Age/4 + 3.5; uncuffed only in children under 8 years
options.D: ETT diameter is weight-based; use uncuffed ETT at all ages in pre-hospital setting to minimise airway trauma
options.E: ETT internal diameter (mm) = (Age + 16)/4; cuffed ETTs are contraindicated under age 10

explanation: The standard formula for paediatric ETT sizing is: internal diameter (mm) = age/4 + 4 (or age/4 + 3.5 for cuffed ETTs). Historical teaching that cuffed ETTs should be avoided in children under 8 has been revised — modern evidence and APLS (7th edition 2021) support the use of cuffed ETTs in all age groups including infants, provided an appropriate size is chosen and cuff pressure is monitored and maintained <25 cmH₂O (to avoid tracheal mucosal ischaemia). Cuffed ETTs offer important advantages in retrieval: they prevent aspiration, reduce gas leakage (improving ventilation accuracy), and reduce the need for tube changes. A critical retrieval-specific consideration is altitude physiology: as per Boyle's Law, gas in the ETT cuff expands at altitude (as atmospheric pressure falls), potentially increasing cuff pressure to dangerous levels and causing tracheal mucosal injury. Cuff pressure must be checked and adjusted before and during air transport. The Oxford Specialist Handbook explicitly notes this. Using water instead of air to inflate the cuff eliminates this problem, as liquids are not compressible. Option C and D reflect outdated guidance. Option A lacks the altitude consideration. Option E uses an incorrect formula.

text: A 6-month-old infant weighing 7 kg is retrieved for viral bronchiolitis with increasing work of breathing, SpO₂ 88% on 2L nasal oxygen, and a respiratory rate of 72 breaths/min. The referring team asks whether to intubate before transfer. What is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.A: Intubate immediately — all SpO₂ <92% in infants requires intubation
options.B: Trial of high-flow nasal prong therapy (HFNP) at 2 ml/kg/min (14 L/min), targeting SpO₂ >92–93%; reassess work of breathing and respiratory rate — proceed to intubation if no improvement or deterioration
options.C: CPAP via nasal mask 5–8 cmH₂O, with intubation only if apnoea occurs
options.D: Intubate — bronchiolitis with HR >160 and SpO₂ <90% mandates intubation regardless of response to non-invasive therapy
options.E: Increase nasal oxygen to 4L/min and reassess over 30 minutes before deciding

explanation: High-flow nasal prong therapy (HFNP, also called high-flow humidified oxygen therapy) has become the primary escalation step for moderate-severe bronchiolitis in infants prior to intubation, supported by NICE guideline NG9 (Bronchiolitis 2015), the PICU network guidelines across UK and Ireland, and the PARIS and TRAMONTANE trials. The recommended flow rate in children is 2 ml/kg/min (so 2 × 7 = 14 L/min for this infant). HFNP provides: (1) reduction of nasopharyngeal dead space; (2) warmed, humidified gas improving mucociliary clearance; (3) a variable degree of CPAP. In bronchiolitis, HFNP has been shown to reduce the rate of intubation and ICU admission in several RCTs. Important retrieval considerations: HFNP consumes significant oxygen volumes; oxygen supply for transfer must be calculated carefully. Signs mandating intubation regardless of HFNP response: apnoea, worsening consciousness, exhaustion, progressive hypercapnia. CPAP (option C) is an appropriate alternative but HFNP is more standard first-line in bronchiolitis. Intubating without a trial of HFNP (option A/D) is not evidence-based in a child who is maintaining consciousness and has not yet had escalation therapy.

text: A 3-year-old child requires emergency IO access during retrieval. The EZIO drill system is used and the IO is placed in the proximal tibia. Which of the following is the MOST important check to confirm correct placement before use?

options.A: X-ray confirmation of IO needle position
options.B: Aspiration of blood/marrow, absence of extravasation, and free flow of 5–10 ml flush with no swelling
options.C: Fluoroscopy to exclude cortex penetration
options.D: Measurement of IO pressure to confirm intramedullary positioning
options.E: The IO can be used immediately after insertion regardless of confirmation steps

explanation: Intraosseous access confirmation in the retrieval setting does not routinely require imaging. The clinical confirmation criteria are: (1) Needle is firmly placed and does not wobble; (2) Blood or marrow can be aspirated (note: marrow aspiration is not always achieved, particularly with small paediatric bones, but its presence confirms placement); (3) Flush of 5–10 ml normal saline flows freely without resistance; (4) No extravasation or soft tissue swelling at the insertion site; (5) The patient appears comfortable with infusion (awake patients experience significant pain with IO infusion into marrow — use lignocaine 0.5 mg/kg IO bolus before flushing if the patient is conscious). X-ray (option A) and fluoroscopy (option C) are not available or practical in the retrieval environment and would delay treatment. IO pressure measurement (option D) is not standard clinical practice. Option E is dangerous — undetected malposition risks compartment syndrome or medication extravasation. APLS (7th edition) and RCPCH paediatric emergency guidelines confirm these bedside confirmation criteria.

text: A 19-year-old male is retrieved following a road traffic collision with a GCS of 8 (E2V2M4), bilateral fixed dilated pupils, and a CT showing diffuse axonal injury with no haematoma requiring surgery. BP is 90/65 mmHg, HR 118 bpm. What is the correct blood pressure management target during retrieval, and why?

options.A: Systolic BP >90 mmHg — hypotension defined as <90 mmHg is the threshold for secondary brain injury
options.B: MAP ≥80 mmHg — recommended target in severe TBI to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure ≥60 mmHg assuming ICP of approximately 20 mmHg
options.C: SBP should be maintained between 100–110 mmHg — higher targets worsen cerebral oedema
options.D: BP does not need active management unless MAP <55 mmHg in diffuse injury
options.E: Systolic BP should be maintained >110 mmHg in patients aged <50 years per BTF guidelines

explanation: The Brain Trauma Foundation (BTF) 4th edition guidelines (2016), which represent the international standard referenced in UK and Irish TBI management protocols including NICE CG176, provide age-stratified thresholds for minimum systolic blood pressure in TBI: SBP ≥110 mmHg for patients aged 15–49 years and ≥70 years; SBP ≥100 mmHg for patients aged 50–69 years. These thresholds represent the minimum below which secondary brain injury risk is substantially increased. The previous threshold of SBP >90 mmHg (option A) has been superseded — evidence shows that even brief episodes below 110 mmHg in young adults with severe TBI independently worsen outcomes. This patient at 90/65 mmHg requires urgent fluid resuscitation and vasopressor support to achieve SBP ≥110 mmHg. MAP ≥80 mmHg (option B) is reasonable but the BTF specifies SBP thresholds rather than MAP for their published evidence-based recommendations. Option C is incorrect — higher BP targets are appropriate to maintain cerebral perfusion. Option D massively underestimates the safe threshold.

text: During a TBI retrieval, the transport ventilator ETCO₂ monitor shows a rising ETCO₂ reading from 4.5 kPa to 6.8 kPa. The patient simultaneously develops flexor posturing. What is the most appropriate immediate response, and what is the likely underlying cause?

options.A: Increase respiratory rate from 14 to 20 to reduce PaCO₂; posturing is due to pain
options.B: Check ETT position and patency, increase respiratory rate to target ETCO₂ 4.0–4.5 kPa, treat suspected acute rise in ICP; consider IV mannitol 0.25–0.5 g/kg or 3% hypertonic saline if available
options.C: Administer IV diazepam 5 mg for seizure activity
options.D: Reduce PEEP to decrease intrathoracic pressure and improve cerebral venous drainage
options.E: Stop the vehicle and arrange emergency CT scan before any treatment

explanation: Rising ETCO₂ with flexor posturing in a severe TBI patient represents acute neurological deterioration likely due to rising ICP — the classic Cushing response (hypertension, bradycardia, abnormal posturing) may follow. Management priorities: (1) Confirm ETT position and patency — ETT displacement or obstruction could cause hypercapnia; (2) Increase respiratory rate to target normocapnia/mild hypocapnia (ETCO₂ 4.0–4.5 kPa, PaCO₂ 4.5–5.0 kPa) — brief mild hyperventilation (ETCO₂ 3.5–4.0 kPa) is acceptable as a temporising measure for impending herniation only; (3) Osmotherapy: mannitol 0.25–0.5 g/kg IV bolus or hypertonic saline (3% NaCl, 2–3 ml/kg) is the first-line pharmacological intervention for acute ICP crisis; (4) Position: 30° head elevation, midline positioning; (5) Ensure adequate sedation/NMB. NICE CG176, BTF guidelines (4th edition), and FICM TBI transfer guidance all recommend these interventions for acute ICP crisis. Diazepam (option C) — posturing is not seizure activity. Reducing PEEP (option D) may help cerebral venous drainage but is not the primary intervention. Option E — CT cannot be performed en route and delay is dangerous.

text: A 24-year-old female is retrieved following a high-speed horse-riding fall with suspected cervical spine injury (CXR shows widening of C5/6 disc space). She is conscious, GCS 15, with paraesthesia in both upper limbs. She is currently in a cervical hard collar. What additional spinal immobilisation strategy is most appropriate for road transfer?

options.A: Hard collar alone is sufficient — full spinal board immobilisation is no longer recommended
options.B: Hard collar plus head blocks and straps, full spinal board, with log roll technique for any position changes
options.C: Hard collar plus vacuum mattress conforming to body contours, with straps across chest and legs; no rigid board required
options.D: Hard collar and manual in-line stabilisation throughout transfer by a dedicated team member
options.E: Remove the hard collar as it restricts venous drainage and increases ICP — use a vacuum mattress only

explanation: Current spinal immobilisation guidance has evolved significantly. JRCALC (2021 edition), the Faculty of Pre-Hospital Care, and RCEM guidance now recommend that vacuum mattresses are preferred over rigid spinal boards for inter-hospital and retrieval transfers because they: (1) conform to the patient's body, distributing pressure more evenly and reducing risk of pressure sores; (2) provide superior immobilisation of the spine compared to long boards; (3) are more comfortable for conscious patients. Full spinal board immobilisation for extended transfers (option B) is no longer best practice and is associated with pressure injury, pain, and respiratory embarrassment. The combination of hard collar + vacuum mattress + straps (option C) represents current UK and Irish prehospital and retrieval standard. Hard collar alone (option A) is insufficient. Manual in-line stabilisation throughout transfer (option D) is impractical for a road journey and not sustainable. Removing the hard collar (option E) may eventually be appropriate after imaging confirmation, but not during active transport with suspected injury. This reflects both the NICE CG176 guidance and the JRCALC clinical practice guidelines.

text: A 55-year-old male cyclist is retrieved following a high-velocity impact with an unstable pelvic fracture confirmed on plain X-ray. He is haemodynamically compromised: BP 88/55 mmHg, HR 135 bpm. A pelvic binder has been applied by the referring team. Which of the following is the most important additional intervention before transfer?

options.A: Remove the pelvic binder as it may worsen bleeding by increasing intraperitoneal pressure
options.B: Aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation — 2L normal saline bolus before departure
options.C: Activate the major haemorrhage protocol, prepare blood products for transfer, maintain pelvic binder, and pursue damage control resuscitation
options.D: Insert a urinary catheter to monitor fluid balance before transfer
options.E: Arrange CT pelvis before transfer to characterise the fracture pattern

explanation: Unstable pelvic fractures are a leading cause of haemorrhagic shock-related death in trauma. The pelvis can accommodate 3–4+ litres of blood. The pelvic binder (or sheet wrapped tightly around the greater trochanters) reduces pelvic volume, tamponades venous bleeding, and should remain in place throughout transfer — it must NOT be removed until the patient is in a facility capable of definitive haemorrhage control (angioembolisation or surgical packing). Damage control resuscitation (1:1:1 blood product ratio, tranexamic acid within 3 hours of injury) is essential. Major haemorrhage protocol activation ensures blood products are available for transfer. Excessive crystalloid (option B) dilutes coagulation factors and worsens the lethal triad — NICE NG39 (Major Trauma 2023) specifically advises against large crystalloid boluses in haemorrhagic shock, recommending a target SBP of 80–90 mmHg (permissive hypotension) in penetrating trauma; the evidence also supports permissive hypotension in blunt trauma with haemorrhagic shock pending definitive haemorrhage control. Inserting a catheter (option D) is a valid monitoring step but requires care with urethral injury — it is not the priority. CT (option E) should not delay transfer of a haemodynamically unstable patient.

text: A 42-year-old male requires retrieval from an island facility. He has fever, lymphadenopathy, and a rash consistent with suspected monkeypox (mpox). The retrieval team is asked to transport him to an infectious disease unit. What is the correct personal protective equipment (PPE) for this retrieval?

options.A: Standard universal precautions (gloves and apron) — mpox is not transmissible during transport
options.B: FFP3 respirator, fluid-resistant gown, double gloves, eye protection, and apron — treating as airborne and contact precautions
options.C: Surgical face mask, single gloves, and apron — droplet and contact precautions
options.D: Full CBRN suit with self-contained breathing apparatus
options.E: PPE is not required if the patient has no active skin lesions

explanation: Mpox (formerly monkeypox) is a viral zoonosis with human-to-human transmission via: (1) direct contact with skin lesions, bodily fluids, respiratory secretions; (2) contaminated materials; (3) potentially short-range respiratory droplets/aerosols during close contact. The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) and Health Protection Surveillance Centre (HPSC) Ireland guidance for healthcare workers managing suspected or confirmed mpox cases recommends: FFP3 respirator (due to potential aerosol generation during coughing/procedures); fluid-resistant long-sleeved gown; double gloves; eye protection (goggles or face shield); and apron. This is categorised as enhanced contact and respiratory precautions. Standard precautions (option A) are grossly inadequate. Option C (surgical mask) provides insufficient respiratory protection for close-contact aerosol-generating situations. CBRN suits (option D) are not indicated for mpox. Option E is incorrect — transmission can occur even before overt lesions develop, and respiratory secretions are infectious. This question tests DipRTM subdomain 1.11 (management of patients with contagious disease).

text: During a retrieval for a patient with suspected pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) who requires transfer to an infectious disease unit, which of the following represents the safest transport arrangement?

options.A: Transport in a standard road ambulance with the windows closed to prevent exposure to other vehicles
options.B: Transport in a road ambulance with negative pressure capability if available; if unavailable, maximise ventilation with windows open and direct airflow away from the crew; crew wear FFP3 respirators
options.C: Air transport is preferred as altitude reduces airborne pathogen transmission
options.D: Surgical face mask on the patient is sufficient — no additional PPE needed for the crew
options.E: Delay transport until three successive sputum smears are confirmed negative

explanation: Pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) is an airborne infection — infectious particles (droplet nuclei <5µm) remain suspended in air for hours. Safe transport guidance from UKHSA, HPSC Ireland, WHO, and the ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine recommends: (1) Place a surgical mask on the patient (source control); (2) Transport in a vehicle with negative pressure capability if available (rare in most services); if unavailable, maximise vehicle ventilation by opening windows and ensuring airflow moves from the crew compartment forward (not from patient towards crew); (3) Crew wear FFP3 particulate respirators throughout; (4) Minimise the number of personnel in the vehicle. Air transport at altitude (option C) — pressurised aircraft cabins recirculate air, creating a higher risk environment; low-altitude unpressurised aircraft have better throughput but are not preferred for infectious patients. Surgical mask on patient alone (option D) reduces but does not eliminate transmission risk and crew protection is still required. Delaying transfer (option E) is often not clinically appropriate and would be impractical — untreated TB patients often require immediate specialist management.

text: A 35-year-old kitchen worker sustains 20% TBSA full-thickness burns to the face, neck, and anterior chest in a chip pan fire. He has singeing of nasal hair, carbonaceous sputum, and a hoarse voice. His SpO₂ is 97% on room air. What is the MOST urgent clinical priority before transfer?

options.A: Initiate the Parkland Formula fluid resuscitation and confirm adequate IV access
options.B: Apply cling film to burned surfaces and administer IV morphine for analgesia
options.C: Secure the airway with RSI before any oedema progresses — delay risks a 'cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate' scenario
options.D: Apply a non-rebreathe mask at 15L/min oxygen and transfer urgently
options.E: Obtain a chest X-ray to quantify inhalation injury before intubating

explanation: This patient has multiple features of inhalation injury and early supraglottic airway compromise: facial and neck burns, singed nasal hair, carbonaceous sputum, and hoarse voice. Hoarseness indicates supraglottic oedema is already occurring. Burns to the face and anterior neck cause progressive oedema that can convert an initially patent airway into a complete obstruction within hours — often faster than expected. The DAS guidelines, British Burn Association (BBA) standards, ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine, and the Oxford Specialist Handbook all emphasise: early definitive airway securement by RSI in the presence of any signs of airway involvement. A current SpO₂ of 97% provides false reassurance — the airway will not improve, only worsen.
