Delaying intubation risks a CICO scenario where neither intubation nor ventilation may be achievable without surgical airway. The window for safe RSI closes as oedema progresses. Cling film and analgesia (option B) are important wound care steps but not the priority here. CXR (option E) would delay definitive airway management and does not assess supraglottic oedema. NICE burns guidance and BBA both state: "if there is any concern about the airway, intubate early."

text: When applying the Lund and Browder chart for TBSA estimation in a burns patient, why is it preferred over the Wallace Rule of Nines, particularly in paediatric burns?

options.A: The Lund and Browder chart is easier to apply in the field and requires no measurements
options.B: The Lund and Browder chart adjusts for age-related proportional differences in body surface area — particularly the head (larger relative TBSA in infants) and legs (proportionally smaller in infants) — making it more accurate across all age groups
options.C: The Rule of Nines is validated only for adult males and cannot be used in females
options.D: The Lund and Browder chart includes depth assessment alongside TBSA, providing a combined severity score
options.E: There is no clinically significant difference between the two tools — choice is based on clinician familiarity

explanation: The Wallace Rule of Nines assigns fixed surface area percentages: head and neck 9%, each arm 9%, each leg 18%, anterior trunk 18%, posterior trunk 18%, perineum 1%. This is accurate for adults but systematically overestimates TBSA in children because an infant's head represents approximately 18–19% of BSA (compared to 9% in adults), while the legs are proportionally smaller. Using the Rule of Nines in children would over-calculate TBSA from head burns and under-calculate from leg burns. The Lund and Browder chart provides age-adjusted percentages for each body area, corrected for age — it is the recommended tool in BBA standards, NICE, and APLS for accurate paediatric burns TBSA assessment. Both tools should not count erythema (superficial burns) as part of the TBSA calculation. The palm of the patient's hand (including fingers) represents approximately 1% of that individual's TBSA — useful for estimating small or scattered burns. The patient's own palm (not the clinician's) should be used.

text: A 28-year-old male with no known psychiatric history presents to a district general hospital via police escort following a suspected drug-induced psychotic episode. He has been intermittently violent and was partially sedated with IM haloperidol 5 mg 30 minutes ago. He is still agitated, with HR 148 bpm, temperature 38.9°C, SpO₂ 95%, and diaphoresis. The retrieval team is called. Which potentially life-threatening diagnosis must be actively excluded before transfer?

options.A: Thyrotoxic storm
options.B: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)
options.C: Excited delirium with drug-induced hyperthermia
options.D: All of the above — B and C particularly, as both are potentially fatal and treatment differs significantly
options.E: Alcohol withdrawal seizures

explanation: This clinical picture — agitation, hyperthermia, tachycardia, diaphoresis in the context of antipsychotic medication — demands active consideration of several life-threatening diagnoses: (1) Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS): caused by dopamine blockade from antipsychotics (e.g., haloperidol just administered); features are hyperthermia, rigidity (may be subtle early), diaphoresis, altered consciousness, tachycardia, elevated CK — treatment: stop antipsychotic, supportive care, consider dantrolene; (2) Excited delirium with drug-induced hyperthermia: commonly associated with stimulant drugs — potentially fatal without rapid sedation and cooling; (3) Serotonin syndrome: if any serotonergic drugs were taken — features include clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia — treatment differs from NMS; (4) Thyrotoxic storm: important to exclude but less likely in this context. The critical distinction matters because haloperidol would worsen NMS, while benzodiazepines or dantrolene would be appropriate. The NMS diagnostic criteria include: exposure to dopamine antagonist, hyperthermia >38°C, muscle rigidity, autonomic instability, elevated CK. The correct response in retrieval is to recognise this as a diagnostic emergency, avoid further antipsychotics, ensure active cooling, and pursue definitive investigation and management at the destination. RCEM and the Oxford Specialist Handbook both emphasise this diagnostic complexity.

text: A patient with acute behavioural disturbance is sedated for retrieval using IM ketamine 5 mg/kg. Twenty minutes into a road transfer, the crew notes the patient is increasingly bradycardic (HR 44 bpm) and hypotensive (BP 80/40 mmHg). Which diagnosis should be at the top of the differential list in this context?

options.A: Ketamine-induced cardiovascular depression
options.B: Pulmonary embolism
options.C: Serotonin syndrome
options.D: Physical exhaustion-related vasovagal syncope
options.E: Unrecognised co-ingestion of opioids, sedatives, or cardiac agents causing cardiovascular depression — the agitation may have been a manifestation of stimulant drug intoxication that has now worn off

explanation: Ketamine at standard dissociative or sedative doses (4–6 mg/kg IM) characteristically maintains or increases heart rate and blood pressure through sympathomimetic effects (inhibition of catecholamine reuptake). Ketamine-induced cardiovascular depression (option A) is extremely rare at these doses and typically occurs only at very high doses or in catecholamine-depleted states. The combination of profound bradycardia AND hypotension in this context strongly suggests unrecognised co-ingestion of cardio-depressant agents — opioids, benzodiazepines, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, alpha-2 agonists, or alcohol — which may have been masked by the agitation during the excitatory phase of stimulant drug use but are now clinically apparent as the stimulant effect wanes. This is a well-recognised and potentially fatal pitfall in managing drug-induced acute behavioural disturbance. Management: ABCDE approach, IV fluids, naloxone if opioid suspected, consider calcium gluconate for CCB toxicity, vasopressors if hypotension refractory. Always request a toxicology screen. This principle is emphasised in TOXBASE guidance and the RCEM management of acute behavioural disturbance guideline.

text: A 71-year-old male with cardiogenic shock post-STEMI requires retrieval on an intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) from a district cardiac unit to a tertiary cardiac centre. The IABP is currently set to 1:1 augmentation and appears to be functioning correctly on a 3-lead ECG trigger. During road transfer, the IABP begins alarming "poor triggering." What is the most likely cause and how should it be managed?

options.A: The IABP battery is depleted — switch to mains power immediately
options.B: Motion artefact from vehicle vibration is disrupting the ECG trigger signal — switch to pressure triggering mode and check lead attachment
options.C: The IABP catheter has migrated — perform immediate portable fluoroscopy
options.D: The helium reservoir is empty — the pump cannot function without helium
options.E: The patient has developed complete heart block requiring the IABP to be turned off

explanation: IABP triggering relies on a reliable cardiac cycle signal. In the transport environment, vibration from road vehicles commonly generates motion artefact on ECG leads, leading to poor or erratic triggering (the pump cannot accurately identify the R-wave for inflation timing). The correct response is: (1) Check all ECG lead connections and reapply if loose; (2) Switch triggering mode from ECG to arterial pressure waveform trigger — the pressure trigger uses the arterial pulse waveform (from the IABP catheter arterial line) rather than ECG, which is less susceptible to motion artefact; (3) Ensure the trigger mode is appropriate for the patient's rhythm. IABPs in transport should routinely carry spare ECG electrodes and have all leads secured to prevent this. Helium (option D) — IABPs use helium as the inflation gas because its low molecular weight allows rapid movement; it would not suddenly empty without warning alarms preceding complete failure. Migration (option C) cannot be assessed by fluoroscopy in transit. Complete heart block (option E) would require IABP triggering adjustment (e.g., atrial triggering or pressure mode) but not turning off. The Oxford Specialist Handbook and IABP manufacturer guidance specifically address vibration as a transport-specific triggering issue.

text: Which of the following statements about intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) therapy and altitude is MOST accurate?

options.A: IABP function is not affected by altitude as it is a closed pressurised system
options.B: At altitude, the reduction in atmospheric pressure causes the helium gas used for balloon inflation to expand, potentially affecting timing accuracy and requiring adjustment of balloon volume
options.C: IABPs should be turned off for air transport as the risk of malfunction exceeds the clinical benefit
options.D: Only rotary-wing transport affects IABP function — fixed-wing aircraft are pressurised and therefore safe
options.E: The IABP balloon volume must be reduced by 50% for any air transport above 5,000 feet

explanation: The IABP balloon is inflated with helium gas. At altitude, as atmospheric pressure decreases (Boyle's Law: PV = constant at constant temperature), the volume of a fixed mass of gas increases. In an unpressurised aircraft, at significant altitude, the helium in the IABP balloon would expand beyond the calibrated volume, potentially causing: (1) increased balloon inflation volume risking aortic trauma; (2) timing inaccuracy. Most modern IABP consoles automatically compensate for altitude and have altitude-correction algorithms, but this varies by manufacturer and model. The transport team must: (1) Know the capabilities of their specific IABP model; (2) If flying in an unpressurised aircraft at significant altitude, consider requesting a low-altitude flight or sea-level cabin pressure if using a fixed-wing aircraft; (3) Monitor IABP waveform carefully during ascent and descent. Option A is incorrect — the balloon is inflated from an internal helium reservoir that is not sealed from ambient pressure in the same way as a pressurised system. Option C is overly conservative — the benefit of IABP during transport often outweighs risks when properly managed. Option E — a 50% reduction is not a standardised guideline; adjustments depend on the specific device and altitude profile.

text: A patient on veno-arterial ECMO (VA-ECMO) for refractory cardiogenic shock requires transfer from a cardiac surgical centre to a rehabilitation facility 4 hours away. Which statement most accurately reflects the principles of ECLS retrieval?

options.A: VA-ECMO retrieval is a routine inter-hospital transfer that can be performed by any critical care team
options.B: VA-ECMO retrieval requires a highly specialised team with ECMO-specific training, and the transfer should be considered high-risk — ideally conducted by the ECMO centre team or a dedicated mobile ECMO team
options.C: VA-ECMO should be discontinued before transfer and the patient stabilised on maximum vasopressor support
options.D: The primary risk during VA-ECMO transport is oxygenator membrane failure, which can be managed with a spare membrane
options.E: Air transport is contraindicated for all patients on VA-ECMO due to altitude effects on the circuit

explanation: Extracorporeal Life Support (ECLS) retrieval, including VA-ECMO, is classified as one of the most complex and high-risk retrievals in retrieval medicine. The Oxford Specialist Handbook, Extracorporeal Life Support Organization (ELSO) guidelines, and FICM ECMO guidance all state that transport of ECMO patients requires: (1) An ECMO-trained physician and perfusionist (or equivalent trained clinician); (2) Specialist ECMO transport equipment including battery-powered centrifugal pump, portable oxygenator, ACT monitoring, and emergency hand-crank capability; (3) Full critical care capability with backup plans for all conceivable circuit complications; (4) Pre-transport checklist completion by the ECMO team. Transfer of a VA-ECMO patient by a non-specialist team (option A) is unsafe. Discontinuing ECMO before transfer (option C) is likely to result in death in a patient who was refractory to other treatments. Option D — oxygenator failure is a risk, but it is not the "primary" risk (air emboli, accidental decannulation, pump failure, and haemodynamic deterioration are equally or more immediate threats); changing an oxygenator during transport is not feasible. Air transport is not absolutely contraindicated (option E) but requires specific expertise and consideration of gas expansion at altitude.

text: What is the primary physiological rationale for veno-venous ECMO (VV-ECMO) in severe ARDS, and how does this differ from the indication for VA-ECMO?

options.A: VV-ECMO provides both respiratory and cardiac support; VA-ECMO provides only cardiac support
options.B: VV-ECMO provides respiratory support (gas exchange) while preserving native cardiac function; VA-ECMO provides both cardiac and respiratory support, typically for refractory cardiogenic shock or cardiac arrest
options.C: VV-ECMO is used exclusively for paediatric patients; VA-ECMO is the adult form
options.D: VV-ECMO supports oxygenation only; VA-ECMO supports CO₂ removal only
options.E: The distinction is operational only — VV and VA ECMO have identical physiological effects

explanation: This is a fundamental distinction in ECLS: Veno-venous ECMO (VV-ECMO): Blood is drained from the venous system (typically femoral or internal jugular vein), oxygenated and CO₂ removed by the membrane oxygenator, and returned to the venous circulation (typically internal jugular or femoral vein). It provides complete respiratory support — oxygenation AND CO₂ removal — but provides no haemodynamic support. The patient's own heart continues to circulate the oxygenated blood. Indication: refractory respiratory failure (severe ARDS) with preserved cardiac function. Landmark trial: CESAR (Lancet 2009), EOLIA (NEJM 2018). Veno-arterial ECMO (VA-ECMO): Blood is drained from the venous system and returned directly to the arterial system, completely or partially bypassing the heart. It provides both cardiac support (maintains systemic perfusion pressure independent of cardiac function) and respiratory support. Indication: refractory cardiogenic shock, cardiac arrest (ECPR), post-cardiotomy failure. Both modes are covered in DipRTM syllabus 1.15. Option A incorrectly reverses the capabilities. Options C, D, and E are all incorrect.

text: A 44-year-old male is being retrieved for consideration of VV-ECMO at a tertiary cardiac-respiratory centre. He has severe ARDS following viral pneumonitis, with a P/F ratio of 68 on FiO₂ 1.0 and prone ventilation. During the pre-retrieval assessment call, the ECMO centre advises you to initiate ECMO at the referring hospital before transport ("ECMO-run retrieval") rather than transfer on conventional ventilation. What is the most important reason for this decision?

options.A: The patient is too unstable for transport on conventional ventilation — the risk of fatal hypoxia or cardiac arrest during transport outweighs the risk of ECMO cannulation at the referring site
options.B: VV-ECMO is easier to initiate than VA-ECMO and can be done by any experienced intensivist
options.C: The transport time would be less than 30 minutes — ECMO retrieval is always preferred for short distances
options.D: The ECMO team does not have transport capability and therefore cannot transfer the patient
options.E: Once VV-ECMO is initiated, oxygenation immediately normalises and transport risk is eliminated

explanation: The decision to initiate ECMO at the referring site before transport (a "retrieval ECMO" or "ECMO run") versus transfer on conventional ventilation is one of the most important and difficult decisions in ECLS retrieval. The ELSO guidelines, NHS ECMO specification, and Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine identify the key determinant as: can the patient safely tolerate the transport on conventional ventilation? A P/F ratio of 68 on FiO₂ 1.0 with prone ventilation indicates profoundly severe ARDS with minimal reserve — this patient is at high risk of fatal hypoxia during even a short transfer. Placing the patient on VV-ECMO before transport provides a stable bridge: oxygenation and ventilation are maintained by the circuit throughout the journey regardless of position changes or clinical deterioration. The risks of ECMO cannulation at the referring site must be weighed against the risks of hypoxia-related death during transport — in this case, the P/F ratio of <80 despite maximum conventional therapy makes transport without ECMO extremely high risk. Option B is incorrect — ECMO cannulation requires specific expertise regardless of modality. Option E is partially correct but incomplete and misleading as a standalone rationale. This is a pivotal decision-making scenario directly examined in DipRTM 1.15 and represents the most complex tier of retrieval medicine.

text: A 2-year-old, 12 kg child requires retrieval for suspected bacterial meningitis. The referring team ask what dose of ceftriaxone should be administered before transport. Which is correct?

options.A: Ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg IV = 960 mg — should be given immediately without waiting for LP
options.B: Ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg IV = 960 mg — should be deferred until LP is performed
options.C: Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV = 600 mg — only if LP is contraindicated
options.D: Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV = 600 mg — should be given immediately without waiting for LP
options.E: Ceftriaxone 25 mg/kg IV = 300 mg with dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg

explanation: Bacterial meningitis in children is a time-critical emergency where outcome is directly related to time to antibiotics. NICE guideline CG102 (Bacterial Meningitis and Meningococcal Septicaemia in Under 16s, updated NG240 2023) and the UK Meningitis Research Foundation guidance are unequivocal: IV antibiotics must not be delayed for any reason, including lumbar puncture. The recommended dose of ceftriaxone in children (≥3 months) is 50-80 mg/kg/day — for meningitis, this is often given as a single daily dose of 80 mg/kg or divided as 50 mg/kg every 12 hours. Importantly, NICE NG240 specifies the “highest doses recommended by the BNF” (80 mg/kg) IV as a single dose (maximum 4g) for children. For 12 kg: 80 × 12 = 960 mg. The critical message is: do not delay for LP. Dexamethasone (option E) may be given in bacterial meningitis to reduce hearing loss — dose 0.15 mg/kg QDS for 4 days, ideally with or shortly before first antibiotic dose — but it is an adjunct, not the priority. Note for question bank editor: Please verify against current NICE NG240 dose — 80 mg/kg single dose (option B/D) reflects the most current guidance. Consider revising to option D (80 mg/kg, immediately) for accuracy.

text: A 30-year-old woman at 24 weeks gestation requires urgent retrieval for preterm labour with suspected cord prolapse. On arrival, the retrieval team confirms a visible loop of umbilical cord at the introitus. What is the most important immediate intervention before and during transfer?

options.A: Commence IV oxytocin infusion to stimulate uterine contractions and deliver the foetus
options.B: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord continuously (by a gloved hand in the vagina), positioning the patient in the knee-chest or exaggerated lithotomy position, and expedite immediate transfer to obstetric theatre
options.C: Wrap the cord in warm saline-soaked gauze and clamp to prevent further descent
options.D: Attempt immediate vaginal delivery at the referring site using forceps
options.E: Administer IM terbutaline to temporarily halt contractions and transfer in lateral decubitus position

explanation: Umbilical cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency with foetal mortality approaching 100% without immediate intervention. The prolapsed cord is compressed between the presenting part and the maternal pelvis, causing foetal hypoxia. The key intervention is relieving cord compression: a gloved hand is placed in the vagina to manually elevate the presenting part (foetal head or other presenting part) away from the cord — this hand remains in place throughout transport. Positioning: knee-chest position (on all fours) or steep Trendelenburg (head down) further relieves pressure. The bladder can be filled with 500–700 ml saline via Foley catheter to elevate the presenting part if manual elevation is difficult to maintain. CTG monitoring should continue en route. Immediate Caesarean section is the definitive treatment. RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 50 (Umbilical Cord Prolapse) provides these recommendations. Oxytocin (option A) would accelerate delivery but risks further cord compression. Clamping the cord (option C) would be fatal. Attempting vaginal delivery at scene (option D) with cord prolapse is inappropriate without theatre resources. Terbutaline (option E) — tocolytics may have a role in reducing contractions to allow stabilisation, but the manual elevation and expedited transfer remain the priority; terbutaline alone without manual elevation is insufficient.

text: A 16-year-old involved in a high-speed motorbike crash has a confirmed extradural haematoma (EDH) on CT with a 22mm thickness and 12mm midline shift. He has a GCS of 9 on arrival and is now declining (GCS 7). The neurosurgical centre is 75 minutes away. The referring team asks if a burr hole should be performed at the district hospital. What is the correct guidance?

options.A: Emergency burr hole at the district hospital is the standard of care for all EDH with GCS <8
options.B: Emergency burr hole at the district hospital should only be considered if transfer time exceeds 2 hours AND the patient is showing signs of uncal herniation unresponsive to medical therapy — immediate transfer remains preferable in most situations
options.C: The GCS of 7 is not low enough to warrant emergency surgical intervention — transfer immediately
options.D: Medical management with mannitol, sedation, and intubation is always sufficient for EDH pending neurosurgical transfer
options.E: Burr holes should never be performed outside a neurosurgical unit under any circumstances

explanation: Extradural haematoma (EDH) is a true neurosurgical emergency — an estimated 20–35 ml of haemorrhage in the extradural space causes rapidly progressive transtentorial herniation. The British Society of Neurological Surgeons (BSNS), NICE CG176, and the Brain Trauma Foundation all recommend: (1) Immediate transfer to neurosurgery for EDH requiring surgery (haematoma >30 ml volume, thickness >15mm, midline shift >5mm, or clinical deterioration); (2) Medical bridge: RSI, normocapnic ventilation, osmotherapy (mannitol 0.25–0.5 g/kg or hypertonic saline), head elevation, normotension; (3) Emergency burr hole at the referring site: only considered as a last resort when transfer time is prohibitive (>2 hours) AND the patient is in extremis with signs of uncal herniation (unilateral blown pupil) despite maximum medical management. Emergency burr holes performed by non-neurosurgeons carry high risk of haemorrhage and incomplete evacuation. This patient, with 75 minutes to transfer, should receive medical bridging therapy and immediate transfer — this will nearly always be faster and safer than attempting burr hole surgery at a district hospital.

text: A retrieval team is tasked to transfer a patient from a rural hospital who is known to have active pulmonary tuberculosis and is SARS-CoV-2 positive. The transfer is by helicopter. What is the most important additional consideration specific to the air environment compared with road transport?

options.A: There is no difference — the same PPE applies regardless of transport modality
options.B: Helicopter cabins have limited air changes and minimal separation between crew and patient — this increases the risk of transmission. Ensure maximum ventilation, consider N95/FFP3 respirators for all crew, minimise crew numbers, and brief the flight crew on infection control
options.C: Helicopter transport is contraindicated for patients with dual respiratory infection
options.D: The patient should receive pre-flight nebulised bronchodilators to prevent decompensation at altitude
options.E: Positive pressure isolation chambers are mandatory for all dual-respiratory-infection retrievals by air

explanation: Aircraft — particularly helicopters — present unique infection control challenges compared to road vehicles. The helicopter cabin: (1) is small and confined; (2) typically has limited air exchange rates compared to modern ambulances; (3) places crew in close proximity (often within 1–2 metres) to the patient for the entire flight; (4) noise makes communication difficult, which may affect correct donning/doffing of PPE. For a dual respiratory infection patient (TB + SARS-CoV-2), both airborne and droplet precautions apply. Measures: FFP3 respirators for ALL crew including flight crew if proximity allows; patient wears a surgical mask for source control; cabin ventilation should be maximised (fresh air inflow); crew numbers minimised to essential personnel; post-flight decontamination of the aircraft. UKHSA infection control in transport guidance, UK flight operations guidance (CAA), and the Oxford Specialist Handbook all address this. A positive pressure isolation chamber (option E) may be ideal but is not mandatory and is not widely available in routine retrieval practice. Option D — nebulisers generate significant aerosols and should generally be avoided when possible during infectious disease retrievals; alternatively, a spacer device may be used.

text: A 22-year-old male sustains 35% TBSA burns to both arms and hands, chest, and abdomen, predominantly full thickness. During retrieval, which of the following wound care measures is most appropriate?

options.A: Apply silver sulfadiazine cream to all burns before transfer
options.B: Apply moist saline gauze dressings over all burns and cover with warm blankets
options.C: Apply cling film (PVC film) loosely over the burns as a temporary wound covering; avoid wet dressings; actively manage hypothermia with dry warm blankets and warm IV fluids
options.D: Leave all wounds uncovered to allow assessment during transfer
options.E: Apply adherent non-medicated paraffin gauze to full-thickness burns

explanation: The British Burn Association (BBA) first aid and pre-hospital burns management guidance, and the NICE burns assessment guideline, recommend: (1) Cling film (PVC food wrap) as the first-line wound covering for burns — it is non-adherent, transparent (allowing wound assessment), does not shed fibres, and reduces evaporative heat and fluid loss. It should be applied loosely in strips (not wrapped circumferentially around limbs as this may restrict as oedema develops). (2) Wet dressings must be avoided — they cause significant hypothermia in burns patients, who are already at extreme risk of hypothermia due to loss of skin thermoregulation and massive evaporative losses. Hypothermia worsens coagulopathy (in major burns this is a significant risk) and impairs immune function. (3) Active warming is essential: warm blankets over the cling film, warm IV fluids, warm ambulance environment. Silver sulfadiazine (option A) is a secondary wound dressing used in definitive care — it is not appropriate for initial retrieval wound management. Moist saline gauze (option B) causes hypothermia. Leaving wounds uncovered (option D) risks contamination, pain, and heat loss. Paraffin gauze (option E) is appropriate for superficial burns in definitive care, not for retrieval transport.

text: A 66-year-old patient on an IABP is being transferred by road. The IABP alarm sounds "catheter position — check patient" and the waveform shows an augmented diastolic pressure less than the systolic pressure. What does this most likely indicate and what action is required?

options.A: The patient has developed significant aortic regurgitation — stop the IABP
options.B: The IABP balloon may have migrated proximally (toward the aortic valve) — reduce augmentation volume immediately and obtain CXR or fluoroscopy to confirm position
options.C: Diastolic augmentation less than systolic pressure is a normal waveform pattern indicating appropriate IABP timing
options.D: The IABP is over-inflating — reduce balloon volume by 10%
options.E: The ECG trigger is failing — switch to pressure trigger mode

explanation: The purpose of IABP counterpulsation is to augment diastolic pressure (improving coronary perfusion) and reduce systolic afterload. A correctly functioning IABP should produce a diastolic augmented pressure that equals or exceeds the patient's unassisted systolic pressure. If the diastolic augmented peak is less than the systolic peak, this may indicate: (1) Balloon migration toward the aortic valve — if too proximal, the balloon inflates across the renal/subclavian artery origins, reduces effective stroke volume, and may obstruct the left subclavian; (2) Incorrect timing — early deflation reducing augmentation duration; (3) Balloon leak — helium leak reduces effective augmentation volume. In the retrieval setting, the most clinically dangerous cause is proximal migration — this can cause stroke, limb ischaemia, or coronary compression. Immediate actions: reduce augmentation to 1:2 (less frequent), reduce volume by 10–20% as a temporising measure, arrange imaging to confirm balloon tip position (ideally CXR showing tip 2–3 cm below the carina). Significant proximal migration may require stopping the IABP and removing it. IABP manufacturers' guidelines and the Oxford Specialist Handbook both describe this alarm pattern and recommended response.

text: Which of the following most accurately describes the concept of "ECPR" (extracorporeal CPR) and its relevance to retrieval medicine?

options.A: ECPR refers to CPR performed by a trained ECMO perfusionist — it is only relevant to ECMO centres
options.B: ECPR involves the initiation of VA-ECMO during refractory cardiac arrest to restore circulation while the underlying cause is treated — retrieval teams may be tasked to transport patients in arrest to an ECMO centre, or ECMO may be initiated at scene
options.C: ECPR is only indicated for shockable rhythms in patients under 40 years
options.D: ECPR is not currently available in the UK or Ireland and is purely experimental
options.E: ECPR refers to enhanced CPR techniques (compression depth and rate optimisation) rather than extracorporeal support

explanation: Extracorporeal CPR (ECPR) is the use of VA-ECMO to restore systemic circulation during cardiac arrest that is refractory to conventional ALS. It is used as a bridge to definitive treatment of a reversible cause (e.g., massive PE, hypothermia, refractory VF). The ARREST trial (2020, Lancet) demonstrated significantly improved survival with ECPR compared with conventional resuscitation for refractory out-of-hospital VF. ECPR has significant implications for retrieval medicine: (1) "Scoop and run to ECMO" — retrieval teams may transport patients in refractory cardiac arrest to an ECMO-capable centre while providing high-quality mechanical CPR (e.g., LUCAS device); (2) "ECMO at scene" — some ECMO retrieval teams can deploy to the patient and initiate VA-ECMO before transport; (3) This requires: rapid identification of suitable candidates (witnessed arrest, shockable rhythm, no-flow time <5 minutes, refractory after 20 minutes of ALS), coordinated dispatch, and a clear protocol. The ERC 2021 guidelines and NHS England ECPR pathway guidance both address ECPR. It is available in several UK and Irish centres. Option E confuses the term. Options C and D are incorrect.

text: Which of the following most accurately defines the difference between a 'primary retrieval' and a 'secondary retrieval' in the context of retrieval medicine?

options.A: Primary retrieval involves air transport; secondary retrieval involves road transport
options.B: Primary retrieval is from a pre-hospital location to hospital; secondary retrieval is movement from a hospital with limited resources to a specialist care facility
options.C: Primary retrieval is performed by paramedics; secondary retrieval requires a physician
options.D: Primary retrieval is time-critical; secondary retrieval is always elective
options.E: Primary retrieval occurs within the same hospital; secondary retrieval occurs between hospitals

explanation: The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine and the Oxford Specialist Handbook both provide these standard definitions, which are internationally consistent and directly examined in the DipRTM. Primary retrieval: from a pre-hospital location (roadside, remote location, scene of injury) to hospital. Secondary retrieval: from a hospital with limited resources or expertise to a specialist care facility (e.g. district general hospital to a tertiary neurosurgical centre). Tertiary retrieval: from one specialist facility to another (e.g. for bed availability or subspecialty expertise). Quaternary retrieval/repatriation: from a distant or international healthcare facility to the patient's local or specialist hospital. These distinctions matter operationally: the clinical skill requirements, equipment, and team composition differ significantly between primary (prehospital, often chaotic, unstable patient) and secondary/tertiary (inter-facility, more controlled, more time to optimise before departure) retrievals. Option E describes intra-hospital transfer, which is a separate category entirely.

text: A retrieval service is called to transfer a critically ill patient from a remote rural hospital 180 km away. The receiving hospital has confirmed availability. No helicopters are available due to weather. The road transfer will take approximately 3 hours. Which of the following best describes the clinical approach unique to rural and remote retrieval compared with urban inter-hospital transfer?

options.A: Rural retrieval is lower risk as patients in rural areas are generally younger and healthier
options.B: Rural retrieval is higher risk primarily because road surfaces are poorer, increasing vibration injury
options.C: Rural and remote retrieval increases risk due to limited local resources to optimise the patient before departure, extended transit time with potential for deterioration, and greater difficulty accessing additional help in an emergency
options.D: The main difference is the transport platform — rural retrievals always require air transport
options.E: Rural retrievals are lower priority as the patient is already receiving care at the local hospital

explanation: The DipRTM syllabus (subdomain 2.1.2) explicitly contrasts rural, urban, and remote retrieval settings. Rural and remote retrieval introduces several compounding risks: (1) The referring facility may have limited capability to optimise the patient pre-transfer — fewer specialists, limited imaging, restricted blood product access; (2) Extended transit time increases the probability of physiological deterioration and equipment failure; (3) In an emergency during transfer (e.g. cardiac arrest, airway emergency), the ability to divert to a hospital with appropriate resources is limited — the "nearest suitable landing site" may be hours away; (4) Communication difficulties (poor mobile network coverage) may impede contact with the coordination centre. The Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine and ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine both highlight that geographical isolation is an independent risk factor for adverse events during retrieval. The FICM and ICS guidelines on critical care transfer note that retrieval teams must account for extended mission times in equipment and drug planning.

text: During a retrieval mission to a peripheral hospital, the retrieval team arrives to find that the patient is significantly more unstable than communicated in the referral. The team identifies the need to perform RSI and establish invasive monitoring before transfer. Which principle most accurately guides the team's approach to this situation?

options.A: Operate the environment on 'load and go' — any intervention delays definitive care
options.B: The retrieval team should adapt, treating the patient's current location as a temporary ICU, stabilising fully before any movement
options.C: The retrieval environment requires accepting a higher clinical risk than in hospital — proceed with transfer immediately

explanation: Arriving at a scene to find an unexpectedly deteriorating patient is one of the most challenging scenarios in retrieval medicine. The correct approach reflects the core principles outlined in the Oxford Specialist Handbook and ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine: (1) Introduce yourself, form a team, and delegate roles immediately; (2) Contact the coordination centre to update them on the situation; (3) Perform a systematic ABCDE assessment; (4) Address immediately reversible problems (airway, tension pneumothorax, haemorrhage, hypoglycaemia); (5) Stabilise to the maximum extent possible given the environment — the retrieval physician must be adaptable, treating the referring hospital as a temporary critical care environment; (6) Establish clear contingency plans for deterioration during transfer; (7) Do not transfer an unstable patient when the primary problem is immediately correctable — this is the 'stay and play' principle applied to in-hospital retrieval. Option B overstates — full stabilisation may not be achievable but meaningful optimisation is both possible and essential. Option A understates the importance of stabilisation for secondary retrievals. The 'demanding a handover before assessment' (option E) is contraindicated when the patient is in extremis.

text: A retrieval physician is preparing to transfer a deteriorating patient from a small peripheral hospital at 2:00 AM. The local nursing staff are anxious and the only available doctor is a recently qualified house officer who is overwhelmed. How should the retrieval physician most effectively manage this operational environment?

options.A: Take over all clinical decisions immediately and exclude the local staff from the process
options.B: Acknowledge the emotional environment, assign clear roles to local staff appropriate to their capability, communicate the plan clearly, and express appreciation for their work — integrating them into the team
options.C: Expedite departure as quickly as possible to remove the patient from the stressful environment
options.D: Contact the senior consultant on call to take over from the house officer before commencing transfer
options.E: Provide the local team with written instructions and leave them unsupervised while preparing equipment

explanation: This question assesses non-technical skills in a demanding operational environment — a core component of the DipRTM (subdomain 2.1.9). Effective team integration in an unfamiliar environment is a critical retrieval competency. The Oxford Specialist Handbook emphasises: remembering that referring staff are stressed and outside their comfort zone; not expecting the same capability as a tertiary centre team; building a bridge between teams; communicating the plan clearly. Assigning meaningful roles (even simple ones such as monitoring vital signs, documenting medications, or preparing blood) gives local staff purpose, reduces anxiety, maintains morale, and keeps them engaged as effective team members. This is consistent with crew resource management (CRM) principles: shared mental models, clear communication, and closed-loop confirmation. Taking over entirely (option A) may be necessary in extremis but is demeaning and counterproductive in this scenario. Expediting departure without adequate stabilisation (option C) increases patient risk. Options D and E each introduce unnecessary delays or abandonment of the team.

text: A retrieval coordinator is planning the transport of a 185 kg bariatric patient who has had an acute MI and requires transfer to a primary PCI centre. Which of the following correctly identifies the primary transport modality consideration?

options.A: Fixed-wing aircraft is always preferable for bariatric patients as it provides more space
options.B: Road ambulance is always safest — bariatric patients should never be transferred by air
options.C: Weight and size limitations of rotary-wing aircraft (typically 160–180 kg patient weight limit) may preclude helicopter transfer; road ambulance or fixed-wing aircraft with appropriate equipment should be considered
options.D: Transport modality is irrelevant — clinical urgency always takes precedence over weight limits
options.E: The patient's BMI is the sole determining factor in transport selection

explanation: Bariatric patients represent a specific operational challenge in retrieval. Most helicopters have maximum patient weight limits of approximately 160–180 kg (varying by aircraft type), beyond which safe flight is not possible due to weight-and-balance limitations and risk to aircraft structural integrity. For this 185 kg patient, helicopter transport is likely to be contraindicated and the coordination team must arrange either: (1) Road ambulance with bariatric-capable equipment (reinforced stretcher, appropriate BP cuffs, wider loading ramps); (2) Fixed-wing aircraft where aircraft type and weight limits allow. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine and the Oxford Specialist Handbook both highlight bariatric retrieval as a specific challenge requiring pre-mission planning for specialised equipment. Option D incorrectly subordinates safety limits to clinical urgency — weight limits are not negotiable. Option B is too restrictive — fixed-wing air transport of bariatric patients is feasible with appropriate aircraft selection. Patient weight, not BMI alone (option E), determines aircraft suitability.

text: Which of the following best describes the key medical considerations when acting as a medical passenger aboard a small helicopter air ambulance (e.g. EC135 or AW109) during retrieval?

options.A: The medical passenger has full authority over flight decisions and may override the pilot
options.B: The confined space, noise, vibration, and limited access to the patient are the primary challenges; the medical crew must prepare all interventions before boarding and maintain continuous monitoring throughout
options.C: Helicopters fly below the altitude at which gas expansion becomes clinically relevant, so Boyle's Law is not applicable
options.D: Seatbelts are optional for medical crew when actively treating the patient in flight
options.E: The medical passenger should not communicate with the pilot during flight to avoid distraction

explanation: The EC135 and AW109 are the most commonly used helicopter air ambulances in the UK and Ireland. They offer limited cabin space, significant noise levels (requiring headsets/helmets for all communication), vibration, and very restricted access to the patient during flight. Key principles from the DipRTM syllabus (subdomains 2.2, 3.5) and the Oxford Specialist Handbook: (1) All interventions (airway, lines, infusions, monitoring) must be optimised and secured BEFORE boarding — in-flight procedures are extremely difficult or impossible; (2) The patient must be packaged, secured, and monitored with alarms audible above cabin noise; (3) Continuous SpO₂ and ETCO₂ waveform monitoring (not just numbers) is essential as auscultation is impossible; (4) Safety briefing must be received and understood before flight, including emergency egress procedures. The medical passenger does NOT have authority over flight decisions (option A) — the aircraft commander (pilot) has final authority per aviation law. Boyle's Law IS relevant even at helicopter altitudes (option C) — pneumothorax expansion can occur at altitudes as low as 3,000 ft. Seatbelts are mandatory (option D) except for brief therapeutic interventions, and only when safe to do so. Communication with the pilot is an essential safety skill (option E) — medical crew must be able to communicate patient safety concerns to the pilot.

text: A critically ill patient requires aeromedical retrieval on a commercial airline in a specially configured 'air ambulance configuration.' The cabin is pressurised to 8,000 ft equivalent. The patient has a known right-sided pneumothorax that was 15% on imaging 4 hours ago and has been observed without drainage. What is the correct management before air transport?

options.A: The pneumothorax is small and stable — no intervention required before flight
options.B: Request the airline to pressurise the cabin to sea level and proceed without drainage
options.C: Insert a chest drain before flight, as even a small pneumothorax will expand at cabin altitude and risks tension pneumothorax
options.D: Administer supplemental oxygen to compensate for any pneumothorax expansion
options.E: Proceed at low altitude (below 5,000 ft) — this eliminates pneumothorax expansion

explanation: This is a fundamental principle of aeromedical retrieval. Boyle's Law dictates that at a cabin altitude equivalent of 8,000 ft, atmospheric pressure falls to approximately 75 kPa (564 mmHg) compared with 101 kPa at sea level — representing a pressure reduction of approximately 26%. By Boyle's Law, gas volume increases inversely with pressure: a 15% pneumothorax at sea level will expand to approximately 20-25% at 8,000 ft cabin pressure. More critically, even a small 'stable' pneumothorax may tension during flight because the patient cannot be rapidly assessed and treated in a constrained aircraft environment. The Oxford Specialist Handbook, ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine, and FICM transfer guidance all state: any pneumothorax should have a formal chest drain inserted (not just aspiration) before air transport above 5,000 ft — including helicopter transport, which although unpressurised, still typically operates at altitudes of 2,000–5,000 ft. Option B (sea-level pressurisation) is a valid alternative for fixed-wing aircraft that can achieve this, but it does not address the pneumothorax itself — if it tensions, intervention at sea-level pressure in a confined aircraft is still extremely difficult. Option E is partially correct in principle — rotary wing aircraft flying below 500 ft may minimise expansion — but this is neither practical for a commercial airline configuration nor sufficiently safe.

text: Regarding patient positioning and orientation in fixed-wing aeromedical transport, which of the following statements is most accurate?

options.A: Patients should always be positioned head-forward to reduce acceleration forces on the brain during take-off
options.B: Head-rearward (feet first in direction of travel) positioning is standard for most aeromedical transfers as it minimises the effects of acceleration on cerebral venous return during take-off
options.C: Supine positioning perpendicular to the direction of travel eliminates all acceleration-related physiological effects
options.D: Positioning has no clinical relevance as modern aircraft accelerate and decelerate gradually
options.E: Patients with raised ICP must always be positioned head-rearward during take-off to prevent ICP surges

explanation: Patient orientation in fixed-wing aircraft has genuine physiological consequences due to accelerative forces. During fixed-wing take-off, positive linear acceleration (+Gx) occurs in the head-to-feet direction in a head-forward patient — this redistributes blood towards the lower body and feet, reducing cerebral venous return and potentially reducing cardiac preload. Conversely, in a head-rearward (feet-forward) positioned patient, the same acceleration force acts in the feet-to-head direction — blood is pushed towards the head and thorax, maintaining cerebral venous return. The Oxford Specialist Handbook and ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine discuss this: "A patient facing the rear of the craft is susceptible to redistribution of blood towards the lower limbs [during take-off if head-forward]; a patient facing forward is susceptible to redistribution of blood towards the chest and head [during take-off if head-rearward]." Standard aeromedical practice is therefore to position patients head-rearward, which also places them in the safer crash impact position. For patients with raised ICP, head-up positioning (30°) is desirable, which can be maintained regardless of orientation. Option A is the opposite of standard practice. Perpendicular positioning (option C) does not eliminate G-force effects. Option D incorrectly dismisses acceleration physiology.

text: Regarding the physiological effects of sustained positive vertical G-force (+Gz) during helicopter transport, which of the following is most accurate?

options.A: +Gz forces blood centrally, increasing venous return and cardiac output
options.B: +Gz increases blood pressure in the head and may cause intracranial haemorrhage
options.C: +Gz forces the abdominal viscera and diaphragm downward, increasing residual lung capacity but creating preferential ventilation of apical alveoli with reduced perfusion — worsening V/Q mismatch
options.D: +Gz is not physiologically significant at the G-forces experienced during routine helicopter transport
options.E: +Gz causes immediate loss of consciousness due to cerebral ischaemia

explanation: The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine provides detailed analysis of G-force physiology in transport. During sustained positive vertical G (+Gz — downward force, as experienced by standing upright or in normal helicopter transport): (1) The abdominal viscera and diaphragm are pulled downward, increasing the residual capacity of the lung; (2) Descent of lung tissue distends apical alveoli — preferential ventilation of the apices; (3) Simultaneously, perfusion to the apical alveoli is reduced (gravity-dependent perfusion favours bases) — creating V/Q mismatch that is exaggerated in hypovolaemic patients; (4) Blood is directed away from the head toward the lower body. This V/Q mismatch effect is clinically relevant in patients with pre-existing respiratory compromise. Option A describes venous return effects of negative Gz. Option B describes negative Gz effects on cerebral vasculature. Option E — loss of consciousness due to +Gz requires sustained high G-forces (>4-5G) far exceeding those in medical transport. Clinical application: in haemodynamically compromised patients, the position and orientation in the aircraft should be planned to minimise adverse G-force effects.

text: The main rotor of a helicopter produces vibration at approximately 12–15 Hz and the tail rotor at 23–25 Hz. Which body system is most vulnerable to resonance effects at the frequency range produced by the main rotor?

options.A: The eyes — visual acuity is impaired by resonance at 12–15 Hz, causing difficulty reading monitors
options.B: The lungs — resonance causes alveolar rupture at rotor frequencies
options.C: The cardiovascular system — rotor frequency resonance causes irreversible arrhythmias
options.D: The head/neck and thorax — these structures have resonant frequencies in the 4–8 Hz range, making vibration-induced injury most likely at near-resonant frequencies; main rotor vibration is above this but can still cause neck pain and musculoskeletal fatigue
options.E: The inner ear — causing permanent vestibular damage in all patients exposed to helicopter vibration

explanation: Vibration during retrieval is covered in detail in the ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (Chapter 2) and Oxford Specialist Handbook. Each body organ/structure has a resonant frequency — the frequency at which it vibrates maximally when exposed to external oscillation. Key resonant frequencies: Head (whole body/skull resonance): 4–8 Hz; Thorax: 4–8 Hz; Abdomen: 4–12 Hz; Eyes: 12–27 Hz (reducing visual acuity, making monitor reading difficult). The main rotor vibration at 12–15 Hz is nearest to the eye resonant frequency — this causes visual disturbance and difficulty reading monitors. However, the body structure most vulnerable to injury from vibration in the transport context is the head and neck (musculoskeletal fatigue, neck pain) and the whole body, as frequencies near 4–8 Hz (from fixed-wing turbulence and road ambulance) are closest to the resonant frequency of the head. Physiological effects of vibration across all transport platforms include: increased heart rate, increased blood pressure, raised lactate, reduced musculoskeletal efficiency, impaired thermoregulation, and potential interference with monitoring equipment. Eye resonance (option A) is accurate in description but not the primary injury concern. Options B, C, and E overstate harm.

text: Which of the following represents the most important safety principle regarding 'crashworthiness' that retrieval teams should apply when loading patients and equipment onto transport platforms?

options.A: Crashworthiness is primarily the responsibility of the aircraft/vehicle manufacturer and does not impact clinical practice
options.B: All equipment must be secured according to weight-bearing specifications; unsecured equipment becomes a lethal projectile in a crash — kinetic energy is proportional to mass × velocity²
options.C: Patients should not be secured with multiple restraints as this impedes rapid clinical access
options.D: Crashworthiness only applies to fixed-wing aircraft travelling at high speed
options.E: Medical equipment should be stored in the same compartment as the patient to allow quick access regardless of securing

explanation: Crashworthiness in retrieval refers to the design principles and operational practices that maximise survival of occupants during a crash or sudden deceleration event. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine explicitly states: in a short-duration deceleration (crash), any unsecured object becomes a projectile. Kinetic energy is proportional to ½mv² — in a sudden deceleration, even a relatively light object (e.g. a 2 kg monitor) travelling at 60 mph has lethal kinetic energy. Key principles: (1) All equipment must be secured to the aircraft/vehicle using certified restraints that meet aviation or road safety weight specifications; (2) Equipment should NEVER be placed on seats, floors without securing, or balanced on stretchers; (3) Oxygen cylinders and monitors are particularly dangerous as projectiles; (4) The patient must be secured to the stretcher using appropriate restraints; (5) Stretchers must be locked to the vehicle floor using certified locking mechanisms. This is not merely theoretical — multiple fatalities in aeromedical retrieval have been attributed to unsecured equipment. Option C is incorrect — crash restraints for patients are mandatory and do not significantly impede access in a properly designed platform. Options A, D, and E all reflect misunderstandings of the principle.

text: According to Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures, as a fixed-wing aircraft climbs to a cruising altitude with cabin pressure equivalent to 8,000 ft, what happens to the partial pressure of oxygen in the cabin?

options.A: The partial pressure of oxygen increases as oxygen is compressed by the aircraft engines
options.B: The percentage of oxygen in the cabin air remains 21% but the total atmospheric pressure falls, reducing the partial pressure of oxygen proportionally
options.C: The partial pressure of oxygen remains constant as the cabin is pressurised
options.D: The concentration of oxygen in cabin air falls below 21% as altitude increases
options.E: Dalton's Law only applies to unpressurised aircraft

explanation: Dalton's Law states that the total pressure of a gas mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of its component gases. At sea level: atmospheric pressure ≈ 101 kPa; oxygen partial pressure (PO₂) = 21% × 101 = 21.2 kPa. At 8,000 ft equivalent cabin pressure ≈ 75 kPa; PO₂ = 21% × 75 = 15.8 kPa — a reduction of approximately 25%. The fractional concentration of oxygen in air does not change (still 21% nitrogen + oxygen mix) — the aircraft does not selectively remove or add gases. However, the lower total pressure means each breath delivers fewer oxygen molecules per unit volume. This is 'hypobaric hypoxia' — the cause of all altitude-related oxygen effects. For a healthy individual, the sigmoid shape of the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve means SpO₂ falls from ~98% at sea level to approximately 93–95% at 8,000 ft cabin altitude. For patients with pre-existing respiratory disease, this reduction in PO₂ may cause clinically significant hypoxia — requiring supplemental oxygen. Option C incorrectly suggests pressurisation maintains partial pressure — pressurisation maintains a fixed cabin altitude (e.g. 8,000 ft) but this is still below sea level pressure.

text: A patient with a 10% left pneumothorax on chest X-ray is to be transported in a helicopter. The helicopter is expected to operate at a maximum altitude of 3,000 ft (approximately 914 m). Using Boyle's Law, what is the approximate percentage increase in pneumothorax volume at this altitude?

options.A: No change — Boyle's Law is not applicable below 10,000 ft
options.B: Approximately 10% increase in volume
options.C: Approximately 25% increase in volume
options.D: Approximately 50% increase in volume
options.E: Approximately 5% increase in volume

explanation: Boyle's Law: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ (at constant temperature). At sea level: P₁ = 101 kPa. At 3,000 ft (approximately 914 m): atmospheric pressure ≈ 90 kPa. Applying Boyle's Law: V₂ = (P₁ × V₁) / P₂ = (101 × V₁) / 90 = 1.12 × V₁. Therefore volume increases by approximately 12% — the closest answer is option B (10% increase). At 5,000 ft: pressure ≈ 85 kPa → volume increases by approximately 19%. At 8,000 ft: pressure ≈ 75 kPa → volume increases by approximately 35%. The Oxford Specialist Handbook states: "It is estimated that the pneumothorax will expand by at least 10–25% of its original size at 5,000 ft." The clinical implication: even at low helicopter altitude (3,000 ft), a 10% pneumothorax will expand to approximately 11–12%, and at 5,000 ft would become approximately 12–13% — with the additional risk that the expansion may tension given the dynamic flight environment. This calculation supports the principle of pre-flight chest drain insertion for any pneumothorax prior to aeromedical transport. Understanding Boyle's Law calculations is directly examined in the DipRTM under subdomains 2.4.2 and 2.4.3.

text: A patient with known sickle cell anaemia (HbSS) requires urgent transfer by helicopter. Which statement most accurately describes the altitude considerations for this patient?

options.A: Sickle cell anaemia is not affected by altitude as the condition is genetic rather than physiological
options.B: Flying above 300 ft is contraindicated in all sickle cell patients
options.C: The reduction in PO₂ at altitude may precipitate sickling; a pressurised fixed-wing aircraft with sea-level cabin, or a low-altitude rotary-wing flight below 1,000 ft, should be requested; supplemental oxygen is essential
options.D: Sickle cell patients tolerate altitude better than normal patients because HbS has higher oxygen affinity
options.E: A blood transfusion should be given before any flight to prevent sickling

explanation: Haemoglobin S (HbS) has reduced solubility when deoxygenated — as PO₂ falls, HbS polymerises and red cells sickle, causing vascular occlusion, haemolysis, and acute chest syndrome. The Oxford Specialist Handbook lists sickle cell anaemia as one of the conditions requiring a sea-level cabin for fixed-wing air transport. At altitude, the fall in PO₂ (even to a cabin altitude of 8,000 ft, where PO₂ ≈ 15.8 kPa) reduces haemoglobin oxygen saturation below the threshold that may precipitate sickling in vulnerable patients. Management: (1) Supplemental oxygen throughout flight; (2) Request sea-level cabin pressurisation for fixed-wing aircraft; (3) For rotary-wing transport, fly at the lowest practicable altitude; (4) Ensure adequate hydration (dehydration exacerbates sickling). HbS does NOT have higher oxygen affinity (option D) — the oxygen dissociation curve is shifted rightward in sickle cell, reducing oxygen affinity and worsening deoxygenation at lower PO₂. Pre-flight transfusion (option E) is not a standard pre-flight intervention in all sickle cell patients — it has specific indications and risks.

text: What is the 'Time of Useful Consciousness' (TUC) and at what altitude does it become critically short during explosive decompression?

options.A: TUC is the time taken for a patient to regain consciousness after sedation; it becomes critically short above 25,000 ft
options.B: TUC is the time between interruption of oxygen supply and loss of the ability to perform useful function; it falls to approximately 9–12 seconds at 40,000 ft following explosive decompression
options.C: TUC is the maximum permitted duty time for retrieval personnel; it is regulated by aviation authority rules
options.D: TUC is not relevant to retrieval medicine as medical aircraft are always pressurised
options.E: TUC is the same at all altitudes because the human body adapts to hypoxia regardless of the speed of onset

explanation: Time of Useful Consciousness (TUC), also called Time of Effective Consciousness, is defined as the interval between the interruption of the oxygen supply and the point where an individual can no longer perform meaningful actions (e.g. don an oxygen mask). It is altitude-dependent: at 25,000 ft ≈ 3–5 minutes (gradual decompression); at 30,000 ft ≈ 1–2 minutes; at 35,000 ft ≈ 30–60 seconds; at 40,000 ft after explosive decompression ≈ 9–12 seconds. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine includes a table of TUC values by altitude. The insidious danger is that early hypoxic impairment affects judgement before the individual is aware of any problem — an individual may feel 'fine' while already cognitively impaired. This phenomenon (feeling of well-being from hypoxia) combined with a short TUC means that at high cruise altitudes, explosive decompression leaves almost no time to don an oxygen mask before incapacitation. Relevance to retrieval: fixed-wing aircraft used for aeromedical transport may reach 25,000–35,000 ft; understanding TUC informs why emergency oxygen is maintained immediately accessible to all crew and why depressurisation protocols require immediate action. Option D incorrectly dismisses relevance.

text: According to Henry's Law, a SCUBA diver surfaces from a 40-metre dive. How soon should they wait before commercial air travel to minimise the risk of decompression sickness?

options.A: 2 hours
options.B: 4 hours
options.C: 12 hours
options.D: At least 24 hours (ideally 48 hours) following a single no-decompression dive
options.E: No waiting period is required if the diver uses supplemental oxygen during the flight

explanation: Henry's Law: the amount of gas dissolved in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas above the liquid. During SCUBA diving, breathing compressed air at depth increases the partial pressure of nitrogen, causing more nitrogen to dissolve in body tissues. On ascent, nitrogen comes out of solution as bubbles if the pressure differential is too great (decompression sickness). Air travel following diving increases the risk further: the reduction in cabin pressure at altitude reduces the partial pressure of nitrogen in the atmosphere, causing additional nitrogen to emerge from tissues. Divers Alert Network (DAN) and aviation medicine guidelines recommend: (1) For single no-decompression dives: minimum 12 hours surface interval, ideally 24 hours; (2) For multiple dives or decompression dives (which the 40m dive represents as it exceeds no-decompression limits for many dive tables): minimum 24 hours, ideally 48 hours; (3) For dives requiring decompression stops: >24 hours, DAN recommends 48 hours. A 40m dive is near or at the limit of recreational diving — it will accumulate significant nitrogen load requiring adequate surface interval. Supplemental oxygen (option E) does not eliminate the risk of bubble formation from dissolved tissue nitrogen.

text: An intubated patient is being transported at 6,000 ft cabin altitude in a fixed-wing aircraft. The ETT cuff was inflated with air to achieve a seal at sea level. What physiological process occurs at altitude and what is the correct clinical response?

options.A: The cuff volume decreases at altitude due to compression — inflate the cuff further
options.B: The cuff volume increases at altitude per Boyle's Law as the surrounding air pressure decreases, risking tracheal mucosal ischaemia — deflate slightly and monitor cuff pressure with a manometer
options.C: Cuff pressure is unaffected by altitude as the cuff is a closed system
options.D: Replace the air in the cuff with saline before flight, as saline is incompressible and unaffected by Boyle's Law
options.E: Cut the pilot balloon to prevent over-inflation

explanation: The ETT cuff, inflated with air at sea level, contains a fixed mass of gas. As altitude increases and surrounding atmospheric pressure decreases, Boyle's Law dictates that the volume (and therefore pressure) of the air in the cuff increases. The cuff expands against the tracheal wall, increasing cuff pressure. Cuff pressures above 30 cmH₂O can impair tracheal mucosal perfusion, risking pressure necrosis and ischaemia — a risk that increases over extended aeromedical transports. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine states: "ETT cuffs: risk of tracheal mucosa pressure necrosis with altitude gain. Filling cuffs with water is difficult/impractical. If travelling above 2–3,000 feet let some air out of the cuff and monitor cuff pressure with a manometer." Gold standard recommendation: (1) Monitor cuff pressure with a handheld manometer throughout flight; (2) Target cuff pressure 20–30 cmH₂O (AAGBI/RCoA recommendation); (3) Inflate the cuff with saline before flight if this is feasible (option D is correct in principle but acknowledged as impractical in the ABC text); (4) Deflate and re-check as altitude changes during ascent and descent. Option A is incorrect — the cuff expands, not shrinks. Option C is incorrect — the cuff is compressible and affected by Boyle's Law.

text: Which of the following best explains why auscultation of breath sounds is unreliable during helicopter retrieval?

options.A: Vibration from the rotor causes the stethoscope to give false positive breath sounds
options.B: Ambient noise levels in helicopter cabins (typically 85–100 dB) overwhelm the acoustic signal from the stethoscope, making auscultation effectively useless
options.C: The altitude in helicopters changes the acoustic properties of lung sounds
options.D: Standard stethoscopes are prohibited in helicopter cabins due to electromagnetic interference with avionics
options.E: The patient's sedation reduces chest movement, making sounds too quiet to hear

explanation: Noise is a major environmental challenge in helicopter retrieval. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (Chapter 3) defines noise as "sound that bothers and may harm the medical crew and patient." Helicopter cabins generate noise levels of approximately 85–100 dB from rotor and engine sources. Standard stethoscopes are effective at ambient noise levels below approximately 60 dB — in the helicopter environment, the signal-to-noise ratio is so poor that auscultation is effectively useless. Clinical implication: retrieval teams must NOT rely on auscultation for monitoring during helicopter transport. Alternatives: (1) Continuous waveform capnography (ETCO₂) is essential for confirming ventilation; (2) Pulse oximetry with plethysmographic waveform confirms circulation and oxygenation; (3) Chest rise observation and ventilator parameters substitute for breath sound assessment; (4) Electronic stethoscopes with active noise cancellation may provide some limited benefit. Option A — vibration artifact is a real issue but is not why auscultation fails. Option D — stethoscopes are permitted in aircraft. Option C — altitude does not change acoustic properties of lung tissue at clinically relevant altitudes.

text: A retrieval crew member experiences sudden dizziness, nausea, and disorientation during a night helicopter flight over water. The pilot is informed. Which phenomenon is most likely occurring?

options.A: Acute mountain sickness from altitude exposure
options.B: Carbon monoxide poisoning from the aircraft engine
options.C: Spatial disorientation ('leans' or vestibular illusion) — a well-recognised hazard during flight, particularly at night or in poor visibility conditions
options.D: Hypoglycaemia from skipped meals before the mission
options.E: Motion sickness exclusively — managed with antihistamines

explanation: Spatial disorientation in flight occurs when the vestibular system (inner ear) provides inaccurate information about the aircraft's orientation relative to gravity and the horizon. The vestibular system is designed for normal life — it cannot reliably detect prolonged gentle turns, which are common in flight. The result is a series of illusions: (1) The 'leans' — pilot (or crew member) perceives being banked when the aircraft is level, causing a compelling urge to lean to one side; (2) The graveyard spiral — progressive banking during a prolonged turn while the pilot perceives being level; (3) Somatogravic illusion — during rapid acceleration, perceiving nose-up attitude and pitching the nose forward. Night flight and flight over featureless terrain (water, fog, darkness) remove external visual reference, making the vestibular system the primary (and unreliable) orientation input. The Oxford Specialist Handbook and ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine both identify spatial disorientation as a significant safety hazard in retrieval aviation. The correct response: inform the pilot immediately (who is trained to trust instruments over vestibular sensation), maintain instrument flight procedures, and establish a visual horizon reference where possible. This is directly examined under DipRTM subdomain 2.5.5.

text: Night vision goggles (NVG) are used by some helicopter retrieval services during night operations. Which statement most accurately describes their use and limitations?

options.A: NVGs provide full-colour high-resolution imaging equivalent to daylight conditions
options.B: NVGs amplify ambient light to provide monochromatic (green-toned) images; they reduce depth perception, limit peripheral vision, and create challenges for colour-coded medical equipment interpretation
options.C: NVGs are standard equipment on all UK and Irish air ambulances and are mandatory for all night flights
options.D: NVGs eliminate all flight risk at night by providing perfect situational awareness
options.E: NVGs are primarily used by the medical crew to assess patient skin colour in low-light conditions

explanation: Night vision goggles work by amplifying available ambient light (moonlight, starlight) through image intensifier tubes. They provide a monochromatic (typically green) image with significant limitations: (1) Reduced depth perception — loss of the binocular stereoptic cues used in normal daytime depth judgement; (2) Reduced peripheral visual field — typical NVGs provide approximately 40° field of view versus normal human ~200°, creating 'tunnel vision'; (3) Loss of colour discrimination — medical equipment that uses colour coding (e.g. drug labels, cable colours) is difficult to interpret under NVG; (4) Reduced visual acuity in very low ambient light; (5) Weight and balance issues with the helmet-mounted device. The Oxford Specialist Handbook (subdomain 2.5.7) discusses NVG fundamentals. From a medical team perspective: NVG use primarily affects the flight crew (pilots); medical crew must be briefed on NVG use and the limitations it places on the pilot's perception; any tasks requiring the pilot's attention must account for their reduced situational awareness. NVGs are NOT standard on all UK/Irish air ambulances (option C) and do NOT eliminate flight risk (option D). Medical crew may use NVGs in some services but primarily for navigation, not patient assessment (option E).

text: In the context of retrieval medicine, which of the following environmental factors most significantly impairs the ability of the medical crew to detect clinical deterioration in a ventilated patient during transport?

options.A: Temperature changes within the aircraft affecting monitor display brightness
options.B: The combination of noise (impairing auscultation), vibration (causing monitoring artefact), confined space (limiting visual assessment), and lighting (impairing colour assessment of patient) — acting synergistically to reduce situational awareness
options.C: Aircraft acceleration forces, which directly affect the accuracy of invasive blood pressure monitoring
options.D: Changes in humidity at altitude, which affect pulse oximeter probe adhesion
options.E: Electromagnetic interference from aircraft avionics, which makes all monitoring unreliable

explanation: No single environmental factor acts in isolation during aeromedical transport — the critical point is the synergistic effect of multiple simultaneous environmental stressors. The Oxford Specialist Handbook (Chapter 4: Clinical Application of Environmental Factors) lists: confined spaces, light, gravitational forces, noise, vibration, temperature and humidity, and fatigue as key environmental factors. In practice: noise eliminates auscultation; vibration causes artefact on SpO₂, ETCO₂, and ECG traces; confined space limits physical examination (reaching the patient to assess chest movement, checking lines); low light makes patient skin colour assessment unreliable; and fatigue from the combination reduces the team's cognitive function. This cumulative 'environmental stress' is why pre-flight preparation is so critical — interventions that would be straightforward in a hospital become impossible or dangerous in flight. The DipRTM non-technical skills assessment specifically tests candidates' ability to maintain situational awareness of the whole environment (patient, team, equipment, platform) simultaneously. Option C — acceleration forces do affect invasive monitoring accuracy but this is a specific, manageable issue rather than the primary driver.

text: A retrieval team is deployed to a maritime environment to assess a critically ill patient on an offshore oil platform. The only available transport is a winch-capable helicopter. Which of the following correctly identifies the key additional risk unique to this environment?

options.A: Offshore oil platforms are pressurised environments, creating altitude-related risks for the retrieval team
options.B: Maritime helicopter operations including winching carry specific risks: water immersion from aircraft ditching, fire risk on the platform, limitation of safe egress, and the medical crew require specific training and certification (Helicopter Underwater Escape Training — HUET)
options.C: The main additional risk is hypothermia, which can be prevented by wearing standard ambulance uniform
options.D: Radio communication is more reliable offshore, reducing the coordination challenges
options.E: Fixed-wing aircraft are preferred over helicopters for offshore retrievals due to their superior range and stability

explanation: Maritime retrieval introduces environment-specific hazards not present in standard land-based operations. DipRTM subdomain 3.11 and 2.6 explicitly cover maritime environments. Key additional risks: (1) Aircraft ditching — if the helicopter has to ditch at sea, crew and patient are at immediate risk of drowning, cold-water immersion shock, and entrapment; (2) Helicopter Underwater Escape Training (HUET) — mandatory for all personnel operating in environments where helicopter over-water flight is conducted; HUET trains crew to escape from an inverted, submerged aircraft; (3) Fire risk — oil platforms have specific fire/explosion hazard areas; landing zones may be adjacent to flammable materials; (4) Sea state — high seas significantly affect landing pad safety and winching operations; (5) Winching risks — injury to patient and crew from uncontrolled swinging, equipment failure, or rotor wash; (6) Hypothermia — immersion in North Atlantic waters at any temperature can cause rapid incapacitation; dry suits may be required. The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine explicitly lists HUET certification as a requirement for offshore medical retrieval. Standard ambulance uniform (option C) provides no protection against cold-water immersion. Option E is incorrect — helicopters are the primary platform for offshore retrievals.

text: A retrieval team is deployed in extreme cold conditions to retrieve an avalanche victim. The ambient temperature is −20°C. Apart from hypothermia management in the patient, which of the following equipment considerations is most important?

options.A: The transport ventilator will work normally as most ventilators are temperature-compensated
options.B: In extreme cold, battery life may be reduced by more than 80%, fluid bags and syringe pump contents may freeze, and equipment function may be severely impaired — carry additional charged battery spares and insulate all fluids
options.C: The primary concern is ice forming on the rotor blades, which will affect the pilot but not the medical crew
options.D: Cold weather has no effect on medication efficacy in sealed ampoules
options.E: Standard infection control procedures should be suspended as pathogens cannot survive at −20°C

explanation: The ABC of Transfer and Retrieval Medicine (Chapter 3: Environmental Exposure) provides specific guidance on extreme cold operations: (1) Battery life: lithium-ion batteries (used in most portable monitoring and ventilation equipment) lose significant capacity at low temperatures — at −20°C, battery life may be reduced by more than 80%; always carry additional charged spare batteries and protect existing batteries from the cold (insulation, carrying against the body); (2) Fluids: IV infusion bags and syringe pump contents can freeze at temperatures below 0°C — insulate or warm all fluids and use IV set warmers or body heat to maintain infusion fluidity; (3) Equipment function: LCD screens may freeze, touch-screen interfaces may become unresponsive, drug draw-up becomes hazardous with fine motor impairment from the cold, suction devices may freeze; (4) Hypothermia in the patient accelerates coagulation failure, arrhythmias (cardiac arrest threshold below 30°C), and drug metabolism impairment; (5) Personal protective equipment — standard uniform does not provide adequate insulation. Option A is incorrect — ventilators are affected by extreme cold. Option D is incorrect — medications (particularly biologicals and some vasopressors) can precipitate or degrade at very low temperatures.

text: The correct sequence for donning PPE when preparing to manage a patient with a suspected Category A High Consequence Infectious Disease (e.g. Ebola) in the retrieval setting is which of the following?

options.A: Gloves → gown → mask → face shield
